kids.question: What was the name of the baby that the angel said Mary would have?
kids.option_a: Jesus
kids.explanation: Matthew 1:21 — "And she shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins."

kids.question: Who was told in a dream not to be afraid to take Mary as his wife?
kids.option_a: Joseph
kids.explanation: Matthew 1:20 — "But while he thought on these things, behold, the angel of the Lord appeared unto him in a dream, saying, Joseph, thou son of David, fear not to take unto thee Mary thy wife."

kids.question: What does the name "Emmanuel" mean?
kids.option_a: God with us
kids.explanation: Matthew 1:23 — "Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us."

teens.question: Why does Matthew begin his Gospel with a genealogy of Jesus?
teens.option_a: To prove that Jesus is the son of David and the son of Abraham, fulfilling Old Testament messianic promises
teens.option_b: To list all the important people in Israel
teens.option_c: To show that Jesus came from a wealthy family
teens.option_d: To compare Jesus with Roman emperors
teens.explanation: Matthew 1:1 — "The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham." Matthew establishes Jesus' legal right to the Davidic throne and His connection to the Abrahamic covenant.

teens.question: What unusual women are mentioned in Jesus' genealogy?
teens.option_a: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba (wife of Uriah) — all with irregular or Gentile backgrounds, showing God's grace reaches beyond expected boundaries
teens.option_b: Only queens and princesses
teens.option_c: No women are mentioned at all
teens.option_d: Only Mary is mentioned
teens.explanation: Matthew 1:3,5,6 — The inclusion of Tamar (Canaanite situation), Rahab (Canaanite prostitute), Ruth (Moabitess), and Bathsheba (adultery scandal) foreshadows the gospel's reach to sinners and Gentiles.

teens.question: What did Joseph initially plan to do when he found out Mary was pregnant?
teens.option_a: Put her away privily (divorce her quietly), because he was a just man and did not want to make her a public example
teens.option_b: Announce the pregnancy to the whole village
teens.option_c: Take her immediately to the temple
teens.option_d: Leave the country with her
teens.explanation: Matthew 1:19 — "Then Joseph her husband, being a just man, and not willing to make her a publick example, was minded to put her away privily."

teens.question: What Old Testament prophecy does Matthew say the virgin birth fulfils?
teens.option_a: Isaiah 7:14 — "Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel"
teens.option_b: Micah 5:2 about Bethlehem
teens.option_c: Jeremiah 31:15 about Rachel weeping
teens.option_d: Hosea 11:1 about calling a son out of Egypt
teens.explanation: Matthew 1:22-23 — "Now all this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying, Behold, a virgin shall be with child."

teens.question: What does the name "Jesus" mean and why was He given that name?
teens.option_a: It means "Jehovah saves" — He was given this name because He shall save His people from their sins
teens.option_b: It means "mighty ruler" because He would reign on earth
teens.option_c: It means "wise teacher" because He would teach in the temple
teens.option_d: It means "beloved son" because He was God's favourite
teens.explanation: Matthew 1:21 — "Thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins." The name Iesous (Greek) comes from the Hebrew Yeshua/Yehoshua meaning "Yahweh saves."

adults.question: What is the significance of Matthew structuring the genealogy into three sets of fourteen generations?
adults.option_a: The number fourteen is the numerical value of David's name in Hebrew (D=4, V=6, D=4), so the threefold structure emphasises Jesus' Davidic kingship. It organises history into three epochs: Abraham to David (promise), David to Babylonian exile (monarchy/failure), exile to Christ (restoration)
adults.option_b: It was simply a convenient round number
adults.option_c: Fourteen was a sacred temple number
adults.option_d: Matthew was following Roman counting methods
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:17 — "So all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations; and from David until the carrying away into Babylon are fourteen generations; and from the carrying away into Babylon unto Christ are fourteen generations." The gematria of David (דוד = 4+6+4 = 14) underscores the Davidic theme.

adults.question: Why does Matthew trace the genealogy through Joseph if Jesus was born of a virgin?
adults.option_a: Joseph provided Jesus' legal lineage and royal right to the Davidic throne — in Jewish law, legal paternity (not biological) determined inheritance rights. Matthew establishes Jesus' legal claim to kingship through Joseph's line from David and Solomon
adults.option_b: Matthew made an error in the genealogy
adults.option_c: Joseph was actually Jesus' biological father
adults.option_d: The genealogy was added by later editors
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:16 — "And Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus." Note Matthew carefully says Jesus was born "of Mary" (not "begotten by Joseph"), preserving the virgin birth while establishing legal Davidic descent.

adults.question: What does Matthew's use of "begat" (egennesen) throughout the genealogy and its absence in verse 16 signify?
adults.option_a: Matthew deliberately breaks the "begat" pattern at verse 16, saying Joseph was "the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus" — the passive voice and shift in language carefully distinguishes Jesus' birth from natural generation, affirming the virgin birth within the genealogical framework
adults.option_b: It was a scribal copying error
adults.option_c: The word simply ran out of use
adults.option_d: There is no change in wording
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:16 — The consistent pattern "A begat B" breaks at the climax. Instead of "Joseph begat Jesus," Matthew writes "Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom [feminine singular, referring to Mary alone] was born Jesus." The grammar itself proclaims the virgin conception.

adults.question: How does the inclusion of Rahab and Ruth in the genealogy foreshadow a key theme of Matthew's Gospel?
adults.option_a: Their inclusion as Gentile women foreshadows the Great Commission's universal scope (28:19 "all nations") — the Messiah's lineage already includes non-Israelites, demonstrating that God's redemptive plan was never exclusively ethnic
adults.option_b: They were included only because they were famous
adults.option_c: Matthew was required to include all ancestors
adults.option_d: These women were all Jewish by birth
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:5 — Rahab was a Canaanite from Jericho (Joshua 2), Ruth was a Moabitess (Ruth 1:4). Their presence in the Messiah's line shows God's grace crossing ethnic boundaries long before the Great Commission.

adults.question: What does the angel's phrase "that which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost" establish doctrinally?
adults.option_a: It establishes the doctrine of the virginal conception — Jesus' human nature was supernaturally generated by the Holy Spirit within Mary's womb, making Him truly human (born of woman) yet without inherited sin, and truly divine (conceived by the Spirit)
adults.option_b: Joseph was the biological father
adults.option_c: Jesus was adopted by God at baptism
adults.option_d: The Spirit merely blessed a natural conception
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:20 — "That which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost." The preposition "ek" (of/from) indicates the Holy Spirit as the generative agent. This is not adoptionism but incarnation: the eternal Son taking human nature through a miraculous conception.

adults.question: How does the title "Christ" (Christos) in verse 1 function differently from how we use it today?
adults.option_a: In Matthew 1:1, "Christ" is a title meaning "the Anointed One" (Hebrew: Mashiach/Messiah) — it designates Jesus as the long-awaited anointed King, Priest, and Prophet. Over time it became used as a surname, but originally it was a royal messianic title
adults.option_b: Christ was always Jesus' surname
adults.option_c: It was a common Jewish first name
adults.option_d: It was a Roman honorary title
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:1 — "Jesus Christ" = "Jesus the Messiah." Christos translates the Hebrew Mashiach (anointed one). In the OT, kings (1 Samuel 16:13), priests (Exodus 28:41), and prophets (1 Kings 19:16) were anointed. Jesus fulfils all three offices.

adults.question: What does Joseph's obedience after the dream reveal about his character and role?
adults.option_a: Joseph acts immediately in faith — "he did as the angel of the Lord had bidden him" — demonstrating quiet, righteous obedience. He serves as Jesus' legal father, protector, and the one through whom Davidic lineage is transmitted, despite personal cost to his reputation
adults.option_b: Joseph was passive and indifferent
adults.option_c: Joseph resisted the angel's command
adults.option_d: Joseph sought a second opinion first
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:24 — "Then Joseph being raised from sleep did as the angel of the Lord had bidden him, and took unto him his wife." Joseph's immediate obedience mirrors Abraham's faith-response. He becomes the silent, faithful guardian of the incarnation.

adults.question: What does Matthew 1:25 mean by "he knew her not till she had brought forth her firstborn son"?
adults.option_a: The verse affirms that Joseph had no marital relations with Mary until after Jesus' birth, emphasising the virgin birth. The word "till" (heos) indicates the state before the birth; whether the marriage was consummated afterward is not the verse's concern — its purpose is to confirm Jesus' virginal conception
adults.option_b: Joseph never had relations with Mary at any point
adults.option_c: The verse denies the virgin birth
adults.option_d: This is a later textual addition
adults.explanation: Matthew 1:25 — "And knew her not till she had brought forth her firstborn son." The focus of "heos" (until) is to guarantee virginity before and during the birth. Matthew's concern is Christological (virgin birth), not Mariological.

kids.question: Who followed a star to find baby Jesus?
kids.option_a: Wise men from the east
kids.explanation: Matthew 2:1-2 — "There came wise men from the east to Jerusalem, Saying, Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east, and are come to worship him."

kids.question: What gifts did the wise men bring to Jesus?
kids.option_a: Gold, frankincense, and myrrh
kids.explanation: Matthew 2:11 — "And when they had opened their treasures, they presented unto him gifts; gold, and frankincense, and myrrh."

kids.question: Where did Joseph take Mary and baby Jesus to keep them safe from King Herod?
kids.option_a: Egypt
kids.explanation: Matthew 2:14 — "When he arose, he took the young child and his mother by night, and departed into Egypt."

teens.question: Why was King Herod troubled when the wise men asked about the newborn king?
teens.option_a: He saw the child as a threat to his throne and wanted to destroy any rival king
teens.option_b: He was happy to hear about a new king
teens.option_c: He wanted to worship the child himself
teens.option_d: He did not believe in prophecy
teens.explanation: Matthew 2:3 — "When Herod the king had heard these things, he was troubled, and all Jerusalem with him." Herod the Great was notoriously paranoid and had already killed his own sons to protect his throne.

teens.question: What Old Testament prophecy told the chief priests where the Messiah would be born?
teens.option_a: Micah 5:2 — "And thou Bethlehem, in the land of Juda, art not the least among the princes of Juda: for out of thee shall come a Governor, that shall rule my people Israel"
teens.option_b: Isaiah 7:14 about a virgin birth
teens.option_c: Malachi 3:1 about a messenger
teens.option_d: Daniel 9:25 about seventy weeks
teens.explanation: Matthew 2:5-6 — The chief priests and scribes quote Micah 5:2, identifying Bethlehem as the Messiah's birthplace. They knew the prophecy but did not act on it.

teens.question: What did God tell the wise men in a dream?
teens.option_a: They should not return to Herod but depart into their own country another way
teens.option_b: They should stay in Bethlehem forever
teens.option_c: They should go and tell Herod everything
teens.option_d: They should bring more gifts to Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Matthew 2:12 — "And being warned of God in a dream that they should not return to Herod, they departed into their own country another way."

teens.question: What terrible thing did Herod do when the wise men did not return?
teens.option_a: He sent soldiers to kill all the male children in Bethlehem who were two years old and under
teens.option_b: He sent soldiers to arrest the wise men
teens.option_c: He closed the temple in Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He banished all foreigners from the land
teens.explanation: Matthew 2:16 — "Then Herod... sent forth, and slew all the children that were in Bethlehem, and in all the coasts thereof, from two years old and under."

teens.question: Where did Joseph settle with his family when they returned from Egypt?
teens.option_a: Nazareth in Galilee, fulfilling the prophecy "He shall be called a Nazarene"
teens.option_b: Bethlehem in Judaea
teens.option_c: Jerusalem near the temple
teens.option_d: Jericho by the Jordan
teens.explanation: Matthew 2:23 — "And he came and dwelt in a city called Nazareth: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophets, He shall be called a Nazarene."

adults.question: What is the significance of the three gifts — gold, frankincense, and myrrh — brought by the Magi?
adults.option_a: They symbolise Jesus' threefold office: gold for His kingship, frankincense for His deity/priesthood (used in temple worship), and myrrh for His suffering and death (used in embalming) — each gift prophetically reveals an aspect of His identity and mission
adults.option_b: They were simply the most expensive items available
adults.option_c: They were standard diplomatic gifts for any ruler
adults.option_d: They had no symbolic meaning
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:11 — Gold (royal tribute, cf. Psalm 72:15), frankincense (priestly incense, cf. Exodus 30:34), myrrh (burial spice, cf. John 19:39). The gifts anticipate Jesus as King, Priest, and Sacrifice.

adults.question: How does Matthew's "fulfilment formula" function in chapter 2, and how many OT prophecies does he cite?
adults.option_a: Matthew cites four OT fulfilment prophecies in chapter 2 alone: Micah 5:2 (born in Bethlehem, v.6), Hosea 11:1 (out of Egypt, v.15), Jeremiah 31:15 (Rachel weeping, v.18), and the Nazarene prophecy (v.23) — establishing a pattern of showing Jesus as the fulfilment of Israel's story
adults.option_b: Matthew cites only one prophecy
adults.option_c: Matthew does not use fulfilment language
adults.option_d: These prophecies were added later
adults.explanation: Matthew uses "that it might be fulfilled" (hina plerothe) as a recurring formula (1:22; 2:15,17,23). Each citation shows Jesus recapitulating Israel's experience: born in David's city, called from Egypt, mourned by Rachel, raised in obscurity.

adults.question: What does "Out of Egypt have I called my son" (Hosea 11:1) mean in its original context, and how does Matthew apply it to Jesus?
adults.option_a: In Hosea 11:1 "my son" refers to Israel as a nation called out of Egyptian bondage. Matthew applies it typologically to Jesus — as the true Israel, Jesus recapitulates Israel's exodus, succeeding where Israel failed. This is typological fulfilment, not simple prediction
adults.option_b: Hosea was directly predicting Jesus' trip to Egypt
adults.option_c: Matthew misquoted Hosea
adults.option_d: The passage only refers to Moses
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:15 — "That it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying, Out of Egypt have I called my son." Matthew sees Jesus as the embodiment of Israel — the true Son who relives and redeems Israel's story.

adults.question: Who were the Magi historically, and what does their visit signify theologically?
adults.option_a: The Magi were likely Zoroastrian priest-astronomers from Persia or Babylon. Their visit signifies Gentile recognition of the Jewish Messiah — pagan scholars travel to worship while Jewish leaders (who knew the prophecies) remained in Jerusalem. This foreshadows the Great Commission
adults.option_b: They were Jewish rabbis from Alexandria
adults.option_c: They were Roman diplomats
adults.option_d: They were three kings from Africa
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:1-2 — "Wise men from the east" (magoi apo anatolon) were likely Persian astrologer-priests. Their long journey to worship contrasts sharply with the chief priests' indifference (v.4-6), previewing the pattern in Acts where Gentiles receive what Israel rejects.

adults.question: What does the massacre of the innocents reveal about the nature of opposition to Christ?
adults.option_a: Herod's massacre reveals that opposition to Christ is violent, irrational, and ultimately futile — earthly power sees divine kingship as a mortal threat. Herod represents all human attempts to destroy God's purposes, which invariably fail while causing great suffering to the innocent
adults.option_b: Herod was acting rationally and justly
adults.option_c: The massacre was purely political with no spiritual dimension
adults.option_d: God could have prevented innocent suffering but chose not to
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:16-18 — Herod's slaughter echoes Pharaoh's killing of Hebrew infants (Exodus 1:22). Both tyrants tried to destroy God's deliverer and failed. The pattern recurs: human power rages against God's anointed (Psalm 2:1-4) but cannot prevail.

adults.question: What is the likely meaning of "He shall be called a Nazarene" since no single OT verse says this?
adults.option_a: Matthew says "prophets" (plural), suggesting a composite allusion — most likely to the Hebrew word "netzer" (branch) in Isaiah 11:1, connecting Jesus to the messianic Branch, and possibly to the lowly reputation of Nazareth, fulfilling the theme that the Messiah would be despised (Isaiah 53:3)
adults.option_b: Matthew made up the prophecy
adults.option_c: There is a lost book of the Bible that contained it
adults.option_d: It refers to the Nazirite vow of Numbers 6
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:23 — "He shall be called a Nazarene." The plural "prophets" points to a thematic fulfilment. "Nazareth" likely derives from "netzer" (branch, Isaiah 11:1). The town's insignificance (John 1:46 "Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?") fits the despised-servant motif.

adults.question: How does Matthew's application of Jeremiah 31:15 ("Rachel weeping for her children") work as typological fulfilment?
adults.option_a: Jeremiah 31:15 originally described Rachel (buried near Bethlehem) symbolically weeping over Israel's exile to Babylon. Matthew sees a deeper fulfilment: the Bethlehem mothers weeping over their slaughtered sons, with Rachel as the archetypal grieving mother of Israel. But in Jeremiah, hope follows weeping (31:16-17) — pointing to ultimate restoration
adults.option_b: Matthew quoted the wrong passage
adults.option_c: Rachel was literally alive and weeping
adults.option_d: The passage has nothing to do with Bethlehem
adults.explanation: Matthew 2:17-18 — Jeremiah 31:15 sits in a context of hope: "Refrain thy voice from weeping... they shall come again from the land of the enemy" (31:16-17). Matthew's typology implies that even this horrific tragedy exists within God's redemptive plan.

adults.question: What does the pattern of divine guidance through dreams in Matthew 1-2 suggest about God's providence?
adults.option_a: God directs events through five dreams in chapters 1-2 (1:20 to Joseph; 2:12 to the Magi; 2:13, 2:19, 2:22 to Joseph) — each dream steers events to fulfil prophecy while protecting the child. This demonstrates that God's sovereign providence works through ordinary means (dreams, travel decisions) to accomplish His purposes
adults.option_b: Dreams were unreliable and God would not use them
adults.option_c: Only one dream occurs in these chapters
adults.option_d: The dreams were Joseph's imagination
adults.explanation: Five dreams in Matthew 1-2 guide every critical decision: taking Mary as wife, the Magi's departure route, the flight to Egypt, the return from Egypt, and the settlement in Nazareth. God's invisible hand directs visible history.

kids.question: Who preached in the wilderness saying "Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand"?
kids.option_a: John the Baptist
kids.explanation: Matthew 3:1-2 — "In those days came John the Baptist, preaching in the wilderness of Judaea, And saying, Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand."

kids.question: What did John the Baptist eat in the wilderness?
kids.option_a: Locusts and wild honey
kids.explanation: Matthew 3:4 — "And his meat was locusts and wild honey."

kids.question: What happened when Jesus was baptised in the Jordan River?
kids.option_a: The heavens were opened, the Spirit of God descended like a dove, and a voice from heaven said "This is my beloved Son"
kids.option_c: A rainbow appeared in the sky
kids.option_d: Thunder and lightning filled the sky
kids.explanation: Matthew 3:16-17 — "The heavens were opened unto him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and lighting upon him: And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased."

teens.question: What Old Testament prophet did John the Baptist fulfil?
teens.option_a: Isaiah, who prophesied "The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight"
teens.option_b: Ezekiel, who saw the valley of dry bones
teens.option_c: Daniel, who interpreted dreams
teens.option_d: Jeremiah, who wept over Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Matthew 3:3 — "For this is he that was spoken of by the prophet Esaias, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight."

teens.question: What did John the Baptist call the Pharisees and Sadducees who came to his baptism?
teens.option_a: A generation of vipers — and he warned them to bring forth fruits worthy of repentance
teens.option_b: Distinguished guests and honoured leaders
teens.option_c: The most righteous men in Israel
teens.option_d: Friends and fellow workers
teens.explanation: Matthew 3:7-8 — "But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees come to his baptism, he said unto them, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come? Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance."

teens.question: What warning did John give about relying on being descendants of Abraham?
teens.option_a: He said God is able to raise up children unto Abraham from stones — ancestry alone does not save; repentance and fruit are required
teens.option_b: He said Abraham's descendants are automatically saved
teens.option_c: He praised them for their family heritage
teens.option_d: He said only Abraham's children could be baptised
teens.explanation: Matthew 3:9 — "And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham."

teens.question: How did John describe the One who would come after him?
teens.option_a: He is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear — He shall baptise you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire
teens.option_b: He will be a great military leader
teens.option_c: He will be a wealthy merchant
teens.option_d: He will be a political king from Rome
teens.explanation: Matthew 3:11 — "He that cometh after me is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire."

teens.question: Why did Jesus say He needed to be baptised by John?
teens.option_a: To fulfil all righteousness — Jesus identified with sinful humanity and publicly inaugurated His ministry by submitting to God's plan
teens.option_b: Because Jesus needed to repent of sin
teens.option_c: Because John was more important than Jesus
teens.option_d: Because it was a Roman requirement
teens.explanation: Matthew 3:15 — "And Jesus answering said unto him, Suffer it to be now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness."

adults.question: What does "the kingdom of heaven is at hand" mean, and why does Matthew use "kingdom of heaven" instead of "kingdom of God"?
adults.option_a: It means God's sovereign rule is breaking into human history through the Messiah's arrival. Matthew uses "heaven" as a Jewish reverential circumlocution for God's name (avoiding speaking it directly), not to describe a different kingdom — it is synonymous with "kingdom of God" in Mark and Luke
adults.option_b: The kingdom is a physical place in the sky
adults.option_c: Matthew describes a different kingdom than Mark
adults.option_d: It refers only to the afterlife
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:2 — "The kingdom of heaven is at hand." "At hand" (engiken) means "has drawn near" — the eschatological kingdom is arriving in Jesus' person. Matthew's "heaven" (ouranos) substitutes for "God" out of Jewish reverence, as confirmed by parallel passages in Mark/Luke.

adults.question: What does John's imagery of the axe, winnowing fan, and unquenchable fire communicate about the coming Messiah?
adults.option_a: John presents the Messiah as an eschatological judge — the axe at the tree root (v.10), the winnowing fork separating wheat from chaff (v.12), and unquenchable fire all depict imminent, decisive judgment. John expected the Messiah to bring both salvation and judgment simultaneously
adults.option_b: These are farming tips for his audience
adults.option_c: John was describing natural disasters
adults.option_d: The images are purely decorative rhetoric
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:10-12 — "The axe is laid unto the root of the trees... he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire." John's Baptist ministry prepared Israel for this dual advent of grace and judgment.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus' baptism being a Trinitarian theophany?
adults.option_a: All three persons of the Trinity are simultaneously manifest: the Son is baptised, the Spirit descends as a dove, and the Father speaks from heaven — this is the most explicit Trinitarian revelation in the Gospels, inaugurating Jesus' public ministry with divine endorsement
adults.option_b: Only two persons of the Trinity are present
adults.option_c: The Trinity doctrine was invented later
adults.option_d: The dove was simply a natural bird
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:16-17 — Son (being baptised), Spirit (descending as a dove), Father (speaking from heaven). This triune manifestation at the ministry's inauguration parallels the Great Commission's Trinitarian baptismal formula (28:19).

adults.question: What does "to fulfil all righteousness" mean in the context of Jesus' sinless nature?
adults.option_a: Jesus was not baptised for repentance but to identify with sinful humanity, to inaugurate His messianic ministry, and to submit to the Father's will — "fulfilling all righteousness" means completing every aspect of God's righteous requirements, foreshadowing His vicarious role as the representative of His people
adults.option_b: Jesus was confessing personal sins
adults.option_c: It was simply a cultural formality
adults.option_d: Jesus was setting an example for swimming
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:15 — "To fulfil all righteousness" (plerosai pasan dikaiosunen) means to accomplish all that God's righteous plan requires. Jesus' baptism identifies Him with sinners (cf. Isaiah 53:12), anticipates His death/resurrection (Romans 6:3-4), and receives the Father's public endorsement.

adults.question: What does the Father's declaration "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased" echo from the Old Testament?
adults.option_a: It combines Psalm 2:7 ("Thou art my Son") — a royal enthronement psalm — with Isaiah 42:1 ("my servant... in whom my soul delighteth") — the Suffering Servant. This fusion identifies Jesus as both the Davidic King and the Suffering Servant, uniting two messianic streams
adults.option_b: It quotes only from Genesis 1
adults.option_c: It has no Old Testament background
adults.option_d: It comes from Proverbs
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:17 — "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." Psalm 2:7 declares the Messiah as Son/King; Isaiah 42:1 introduces the Servant who will suffer. The Father's words fuse kingship and servanthood in one person.

adults.question: What does the Spirit descending "like a dove" signify?
adults.option_a: The dove imagery recalls Genesis 1:2 (Spirit hovering over waters) and Genesis 8:8-12 (Noah's dove signalling new creation). The Spirit's descent marks Jesus as the bearer of a new creation, anointing Him for His messianic mission as prophesied in Isaiah 61:1
adults.option_b: Doves were simply common in the area
adults.option_c: The dove was the Baptist's pet bird
adults.option_d: It was a hallucination from the heat
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:16 — "The Spirit of God descending like a dove." The dove connects to creation (Genesis 1:2, Spirit over waters) and new beginning (Genesis 8:11, dove with olive branch). Jesus' baptism inaugurates the new creation; the Spirit anoints the new Adam.

adults.question: How does John's statement "I am not worthy to bear his shoes" position Jesus relative to John?
adults.option_a: Carrying or untying a master's sandals was the task of the lowest slave — a task so menial that Jewish tradition said even a Hebrew slave should not be required to do it. John declares himself unworthy of even slave-status before the Messiah, establishing an infinite qualitative difference between them
adults.option_b: John was being falsely modest
adults.option_c: Shoe-bearing was a prestigious honour
adults.option_d: John literally could not lift the sandals
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:11 — "Whose shoes I am not worthy to bear." The Talmud (b. Ketubbot 96a) states a disciple does everything a slave does for a master except removing sandals. John places himself below even a slave — the greatest prophet (11:11) acknowledges absolute subordination to Jesus.

adults.question: What is the relationship between John's baptism with water and the Messiah's baptism with the Holy Ghost and fire?
adults.option_a: John's water baptism was preparatory and symbolic — signifying repentance and readiness. The Messiah's baptism with the Holy Spirit (Pentecost, Acts 2) brings regeneration and empowerment, while baptism with fire represents either purifying judgment or the purging fire of the Spirit. The two baptisms fulfil what John's could only prefigure
adults.option_b: They are identical in effect
adults.option_c: Fire baptism means physical burning
adults.option_d: John's baptism was superior
adults.explanation: Matthew 3:11 — "He shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire." Water baptism symbolises; Spirit baptism actualises. "Fire" likely refers to judgment (v.12, "burn up the chaff") or purification (Malachi 3:2-3). Christ's baptism accomplishes what John's anticipated.

kids.question: How many days did Jesus fast in the wilderness before the devil tempted Him?
kids.option_a: Forty days and forty nights
kids.explanation: Matthew 4:2 — "And when he had fasted forty days and forty nights, he was afterward an hungred."

kids.question: What did Jesus say when the devil told Him to turn stones into bread?
kids.option_a: Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God
kids.option_b: I am too tired to do miracles
kids.option_c: That sounds like a good idea
kids.option_d: I will turn them into fish instead
kids.explanation: Matthew 4:4 — "But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God."

kids.question: What did Jesus say to His first disciples, Peter and Andrew?
kids.option_a: Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men
kids.option_d: Sell your boats and buy land
kids.explanation: Matthew 4:19 — "And he saith unto them, Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men."

teens.question: How did Jesus respond to each of the devil's three temptations?
teens.option_a: He quoted Scripture each time — "It is written" — using Deuteronomy 8:3, 6:16, and 6:13 to defeat every temptation with God's Word
teens.option_b: He used His own reasoning and logic
teens.option_c: He asked the angels for help
teens.option_d: He simply ignored the devil
teens.explanation: Matthew 4:4,7,10 — Jesus responded to each temptation with "It is written" followed by quotes from Deuteronomy. He modelled fighting temptation with the Word of God.

teens.question: What did the devil offer Jesus if He would fall down and worship him?
teens.option_a: All the kingdoms of the world and the glory of them
teens.option_b: A palace in Jerusalem
teens.option_c: The ability to fly through the air
teens.option_d: Eternal life without suffering
teens.explanation: Matthew 4:8-9 — "The devil taketh him up into an exceeding high mountain, and sheweth him all the kingdoms of the world, and the glory of them; And saith unto him, All these things will I give thee, if thou wilt fall down and worship me."

teens.question: Where did Jesus begin His public ministry of preaching?
teens.option_a: In Galilee, specifically in Capernaum by the sea, fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about light dawning on those in darkness
teens.option_b: In Jerusalem at the temple
teens.option_c: In Bethlehem where He was born
teens.option_d: In Egypt where He had lived as a child
teens.explanation: Matthew 4:12-16 — "Jesus... departed into Galilee; And leaving Nazareth, he came and dwelt in Capernaum... That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet."

teens.question: What did James and John leave behind to follow Jesus?
teens.option_a: They left the ship and their father Zebedee and followed Him immediately
teens.option_b: They asked for time to think about it
teens.option_c: They brought their whole family along
teens.option_d: They continued fishing part-time
teens.explanation: Matthew 4:22 — "And they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him."

teens.question: What three things did Jesus do throughout Galilee?
teens.option_a: Teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness and disease among the people
teens.option_b: Trading goods, building homes, and planting crops
teens.option_c: Writing books, training soldiers, and collecting taxes
teens.option_d: Judging disputes, making laws, and governing cities
teens.explanation: Matthew 4:23 — "And Jesus went about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness and all manner of disease among the people."

adults.question: How do the three temptations of Jesus parallel Israel's wilderness failures?
adults.option_a: Jesus succeeds where Israel failed: Israel craved bread/manna (Exodus 16) — Jesus trusts God's word; Israel tested God at Massah (Exodus 17) — Jesus refuses to test God; Israel worshipped the golden calf (Exodus 32) — Jesus worships God alone. All three responses quote Deuteronomy, Israel's wilderness book
adults.option_b: There is no parallel with Israel
adults.option_c: Jesus was tempted in completely different ways
adults.option_d: The temptations only relate to Adam's fall
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:1-11 — Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3, 6:16, and 6:13 — all from Israel's wilderness narratives. As the true Israel/Son of God, He relives the wilderness test and succeeds, demonstrating perfect obedience where the nation failed.

adults.question: What does the devil's use of Scripture (Psalm 91:11-12) in the second temptation teach about handling God's Word?
adults.option_a: Satan can quote Scripture accurately but apply it deceptively — Psalm 91 promises God's protection for those walking in faithful obedience, not for those who manufacture danger to force God's hand. Jesus corrects the misapplication with Deuteronomy 6:16: "Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God"
adults.option_b: Satan cannot quote Scripture
adults.option_c: Any use of Scripture is automatically valid
adults.option_d: Jesus agreed with Satan's interpretation
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:6-7 — Satan quotes Psalm 91:11-12 to suggest Jesus jump from the temple pinnacle. Jesus' counter-quote (Deuteronomy 6:16) shows that Scripture must interpret Scripture — no verse can be used to contradict the whole counsel of God.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus being "led up of the Spirit" into the wilderness to be tempted?
adults.option_a: The temptation was divinely ordained, not an accident — the Spirit deliberately led Jesus into confrontation with Satan, showing that testing is part of God's plan for proving and demonstrating faithfulness. Jesus' victory over Satan inaugurates His ministry with authority over the enemy
adults.option_b: Jesus wandered into the wilderness by mistake
adults.option_c: The Spirit had no involvement
adults.option_d: Satan forced Jesus into the wilderness
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:1 — "Then was Jesus led up of the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted of the devil." The divine passive ("led up of the Spirit") shows God's sovereign initiative. Jesus enters ministry not with a miracle but with a battle — and a victory.

adults.question: How does Isaiah 9:1-2 relate to Jesus beginning His ministry in Galilee?
adults.option_a: Isaiah prophesied that "Galilee of the Gentiles" — a region despised by Jerusalem's elite — would see a great light. Jesus choosing Galilee (not Jerusalem) as His ministry base fulfils this prophecy and signals that the gospel starts at the margins, not the centres of power
adults.option_b: Isaiah never mentioned Galilee
adults.option_c: Jesus chose Galilee for its wealth
adults.option_d: Galilee was the religious capital of Israel
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:14-16 — "The land of Zabulon, and the land of Nephthalim... Galilee of the Gentiles; The people which sat in darkness saw great light." Galilee's mixed population and distance from Jerusalem made it "lesser" — yet God chose it for revelation.

adults.question: What does the immediate response of the fishermen-disciples ("straightway they left their nets") reveal about the nature of Jesus' call?
adults.option_a: Jesus' call carried inherent divine authority that compelled an immediate, total response — leaving nets, boats, and family represents the cost of discipleship. The immediacy suggests a prior relationship (John 1:35-42) but emphasises that Jesus' word demands and deserves complete allegiance
adults.option_b: They were bored with fishing
adults.option_c: They had already sold their boats
adults.option_d: The call was tentative and optional
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:20,22 — "And they straightway left their nets, and followed him... they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him." The pattern of immediate, costly obedience establishes the standard for all discipleship.

adults.question: What does "fishers of men" imply about the nature of gospel ministry?
adults.option_a: The metaphor transforms their existing skill into kingdom service — fishing involves going where the fish are, using appropriate methods, patience, and bringing in a catch. It reframes evangelism as active, intentional work that Jesus Himself will teach them, connecting their past experience to their future calling
adults.option_b: It means they should literally keep fishing
adults.option_c: It refers only to catching criminals
adults.option_d: The phrase was a common Jewish idiom for teachers
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:19 — "Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men." The phrase echoes Jeremiah 16:16 (God sending "fishers" to gather Israel). Jesus redefines their vocation: the same initiative, skill, and perseverance used in fishing will now be directed toward rescuing people.

adults.question: What does the summary statement in verse 23 reveal about the comprehensive nature of Jesus' ministry?
adults.option_a: Teaching (didactic), preaching (kerygmatic), and healing (therapeutic) form a threefold ministry pattern — Jesus addresses the whole person: mind (teaching), spirit (preaching the gospel), and body (healing). This holistic model becomes the pattern for apostolic ministry
adults.option_b: Jesus only taught in synagogues
adults.option_c: Healing was not part of His ministry
adults.option_d: Preaching and teaching are identical
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:23 — "Teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness." Three verbs, three modes of ministry. Teaching instructs the mind, preaching announces the kingdom, healing demonstrates its power.

adults.question: Why is it significant that Jesus' message "Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand" (4:17) is identical to John the Baptist's (3:2)?
adults.option_a: The identical message creates continuity between John and Jesus while also marking a transition — John prepared the way, Jesus inaugurates the kingdom. Both demand the same response (repentance), but Jesus brings what John only announced. After John's arrest (v.12), Jesus takes up the proclamation, fulfilling John's role as forerunner
adults.option_b: Jesus copied John because He had no original message
adults.option_c: The similarity is coincidental
adults.option_d: John and Jesus were teaching different kingdoms
adults.explanation: Matthew 4:17 — "From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand." The verbal identity (3:2 = 4:17) shows Jesus validates John's ministry while surpassing it — the kingdom John announced has now arrived in Jesus' person.

kids.question: Where did Jesus sit down to teach His famous Sermon on the Mount?
kids.option_a: On a mountain
kids.explanation: Matthew 5:1 — "And seeing the multitudes, he went up into a mountain: and when he was set, his disciples came unto him."

kids.question: What did Jesus say we should be like in the world?
kids.option_a: The salt of the earth and the light of the world
kids.explanation: Matthew 5:13-14 — "Ye are the salt of the earth... Ye are the light of the world."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about people who are peacemakers?
kids.option_a: They shall be called the children of God
kids.explanation: Matthew 5:9 — "Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God."

teens.question: What are the Beatitudes?
teens.option_a: A series of blessings Jesus pronounced on those who are poor in spirit, mourning, meek, hungry for righteousness, merciful, pure in heart, peacemakers, and persecuted
teens.option_b: A list of rules that everyone must obey
teens.option_c: A collection of punishments for sinners
teens.option_d: A set of prayers to recite daily
teens.explanation: Matthew 5:3-12 — The eight Beatitudes describe the character of kingdom citizens: "Blessed are the poor in spirit... Blessed are they that mourn... Blessed are the meek..." Each begins with "Blessed" (makarioi) meaning deeply happy or spiritually fortunate.

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the Law of Moses?
teens.option_a: Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil
teens.option_b: The law is no longer important at all
teens.option_c: The law was written by men, not God
teens.option_d: The law applies only to Pharisees
teens.explanation: Matthew 5:17 — "Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil."

teens.question: How did Jesus raise the standard beyond the commandment "Thou shalt not kill"?
teens.option_a: He said that even being angry with a brother without cause and calling someone "thou fool" puts you in danger of judgment — the commandment goes deeper than actions to the heart
teens.option_b: He lowered the standard to make it easier
teens.option_c: He said only murder matters, not anger
teens.option_d: He removed the commandment entirely
teens.explanation: Matthew 5:22 — "But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire."

teens.question: What did Jesus teach about loving your enemies?
teens.option_a: Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you and persecute you
teens.option_b: Only love people who love you back
teens.option_c: Avoid your enemies completely
teens.option_d: Seek revenge on those who hurt you
teens.explanation: Matthew 5:44 — "But I say unto you, Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you."

teens.question: What impossible-sounding standard did Jesus set at the end of chapter 5?
teens.option_a: Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect
teens.option_b: Try your best and that is enough
teens.option_c: Follow the Pharisees' example
teens.option_d: Perfection is not possible so do not try
teens.explanation: Matthew 5:48 — "Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect."

adults.question: How do the Beatitudes subvert conventional expectations of blessing and power?
adults.option_a: Every beatitude blesses those whom the world considers weak or unfortunate — the poor in spirit, mourners, meek, persecuted. Jesus inverts worldly values: true blessedness belongs not to the powerful, wealthy, and celebrated but to the humble, suffering, and righteous. This is a kingdom manifesto that redefines success
adults.option_b: The Beatitudes bless the strong and wealthy
adults.option_c: They describe temporary emotional states
adults.option_d: They apply only to monks and nuns
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:3-12 — Each beatitude overturns human expectations: the poor inherit the kingdom, the meek inherit the earth, the persecuted are congratulated. Jesus' kingdom operates on an entirely different value system than any earthly power structure.

adults.question: What does "poor in spirit" mean, and why does it receive the first blessing?
adults.option_a: Being "poor in spirit" means recognising one's spiritual bankruptcy before God — total dependence on divine grace rather than self-sufficiency. It is first because it is the foundation of all other beatitudes: only those who know their need can receive God's kingdom
adults.option_b: It means being financially poor
adults.option_c: It means lacking courage or confidence
adults.option_d: It refers to being unintelligent
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:3 — "Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven." The phrase echoes Isaiah 66:2 ("him that is poor and of a contrite spirit, and trembleth at my word"). Spiritual poverty is the gateway to kingdom riches — the opposite of Pharisaic self-righteousness.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "fulfil" the Law in verse 17, and how does this differ from "destroy"?
adults.option_a: To "fulfil" (plerosai) means to bring to full expression and intended completion — Jesus does not abolish the Law but reveals its deepest meaning, embodies its demands, and accomplishes its purpose. The antitheses that follow ("ye have heard... but I say unto you") do not contradict Moses but expose the Law's full depth
adults.option_b: Jesus replaced the Law with new rules
adults.option_c: Fulfil means to repeat the Law exactly as given
adults.option_d: Jesus destroyed some laws and kept others
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:17 — "I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil." The six antitheses (5:21-48) demonstrate fulfilment: Jesus does not lower the standard but deepens it from external compliance to internal transformation. Murder → anger; adultery → lust; oaths → transparent speech.

adults.question: How do the six antitheses ("Ye have heard... but I say unto you") demonstrate Jesus' authority?
adults.option_a: Jesus speaks with unparalleled authority — not "thus saith the Lord" (prophetic) nor "Rabbi X says" (scribal), but "I say unto you." He claims authority to authoritatively interpret and intensify the Mosaic Law, positioning Himself as the divine Lawgiver, not merely a teacher or commentator
adults.option_b: Jesus was simply offering personal opinions
adults.option_c: He was contradicting Moses
adults.option_d: The Pharisees gave Him permission to teach
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:21-22,27-28,31-32,33-34,38-39,43-44 — Six times Jesus uses the formula "Ye have heard that it was said... But I say unto you." The authoritative "ego" (I) places Jesus above Moses, the prophets, and rabbinic tradition — an implicit claim to deity.

adults.question: What does the salt and light imagery teach about the Christian's relationship to the world?
adults.option_a: Salt preserves and seasons — Christians prevent moral decay and add "flavour" to society. Light illuminates — Christians are to be visible witnesses whose good works point others to God. Both metaphors assume engagement with the world, not withdrawal. Salt that loses its savour and hidden light both fail their purpose
adults.option_b: Christians should withdraw from the world entirely
adults.option_c: Salt and light are decorative metaphors
adults.option_d: Only religious leaders are salt and light
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:13-16 — "Ye are the salt of the earth... Ye are the light of the world. A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid." Both metaphors require contact (salt must touch food; light must be visible) and both can fail (tasteless salt, hidden lamp). The purpose: "that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father."

adults.question: What does Jesus' teaching on anger (5:21-26) reveal about the relationship between the heart and outward behaviour?
adults.option_a: Jesus traces murder to its root in anger and contempt — "Raca" (empty-headed fool) and "thou fool" (moral reprobate) show escalating contempt. By equating heart-attitudes with the sixth commandment's scope, Jesus teaches that God judges the inner person, not just external actions
adults.option_b: Only physical violence matters to God
adults.option_c: Anger is never sinful
adults.option_d: Jesus was exaggerating for effect
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:21-22 — Jesus moves from the external act (murder) to internal dispositions (anger, contempt, verbal abuse). Each receives escalating consequences: judgment, council, hell fire. The heart is the battlefield; behaviour is merely the symptom.

adults.question: How does "turning the other cheek" (5:39) function in its original context?
adults.option_a: A slap on the right cheek (with the back of the hand) was an insult of contempt, not a life-threatening attack. Jesus teaches not passive acceptance of injustice but refusal to retaliate with violence in the face of personal insult — breaking the cycle of honour-shame revenge without endorsing abuse or injustice
adults.option_b: Christians must accept all forms of violence passively
adults.option_c: Jesus was speaking only about literal slaps
adults.option_d: This teaching applies only to monks
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:39 — "Whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also." A right-handed slap on the right cheek is a backhand — a gesture of insult and social dominance. Jesus' command subverts the honour-revenge cycle by refusing to play by its rules.

adults.question: What does "Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect" demand?
adults.option_a: The Greek "teleios" means complete, whole, or mature — not sinless perfection but wholeness of character, especially in love. The context (v.43-47) defines this perfection as loving without discrimination: God sends rain on the just and unjust alike. We are to love with the same undiscriminating wholeness
adults.option_b: Absolute sinless perfection is required for salvation
adults.option_c: Perfection means following every ritual law
adults.option_d: Jesus was being sarcastic
adults.explanation: Matthew 5:48 — "Perfect" (teleios) means whole, complete, all-encompassing. In context, it summarises the love-ethic: love enemies as well as friends, just as God blesses all people indiscriminately (v.45). Luke's parallel (6:36) says "merciful" — confirming the meaning is wholeness of love.

kids.question: What famous prayer did Jesus teach His disciples?
kids.option_a: The Lord's Prayer — "Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name"
kids.explanation: Matthew 6:9 — "After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about the birds of the air?
kids.option_a: They do not sow or reap or gather into barns, yet your heavenly Father feeds them — and you are worth more than they are
kids.option_b: Birds have to work very hard for their food
kids.option_d: Birds do not need any food at all
kids.explanation: Matthew 6:26 — "Behold the fowls of the air: for they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns; yet your heavenly Father feedeth them. Are ye not much better than they?"

kids.question: What did Jesus say we should seek first?
kids.option_a: The kingdom of God and His righteousness
kids.option_d: A big house and fancy clothes
kids.explanation: Matthew 6:33 — "But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about people who pray loudly in public to be seen?
teens.option_a: They are like hypocrites who love to pray standing in the synagogues and corners of streets to be seen of men — they have their reward already (human praise)
teens.option_b: Public prayer is always wrong
teens.option_c: Only loud prayers are heard by God
teens.option_d: Praying in the synagogue is forbidden
teens.explanation: Matthew 6:5 — "And when thou prayest, thou shalt not be as the hypocrites are: for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and in the corners of the streets, that they may be seen of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward."

teens.question: What does Jesus say about forgiveness in relation to prayer?
teens.option_a: If ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you: but if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses
teens.option_b: Forgiveness is optional
teens.option_c: God forgives everyone automatically
teens.option_d: You only need to forgive your friends
teens.explanation: Matthew 6:14-15 — "For if ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you: But if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about serving two masters?
teens.option_a: No man can serve two masters: ye cannot serve God and mammon (money/wealth)
teens.option_b: It is possible to serve two masters equally
teens.option_c: Mammon is the name of an angel
teens.option_d: Serving money is acceptable if you also serve God
teens.explanation: Matthew 6:24 — "No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon."

teens.question: How does Jesus use the lilies of the field to teach about worry?
teens.option_a: The lilies do not toil or spin, yet Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of them — if God clothes grass so beautifully, He will surely clothe His people
teens.option_b: Lilies are more important than people
teens.option_c: Everyone should become a farmer
teens.option_d: Clothing is not important at all
teens.explanation: Matthew 6:28-29 — "Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither do they spin: And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these."

teens.question: What does Jesus say about worrying about tomorrow?
teens.option_a: Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof
teens.option_b: Planning for tomorrow is sinful
teens.option_c: Tomorrow will always be better
teens.option_d: Worrying helps you prepare better
teens.explanation: Matthew 6:34 — "Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof."

adults.question: How does the Lord's Prayer function as a model for all prayer?
adults.option_a: It establishes a pattern: begin with worship (hallowed be thy name), submit to God's purposes (thy kingdom come, thy will be done), then bring personal needs (daily bread), relational needs (forgive our debts), and spiritual protection (lead us not into temptation, deliver us from evil). God's glory precedes human need
adults.option_b: It is the only prayer Christians should ever pray
adults.option_c: Each phrase must be repeated exactly
adults.option_d: The prayer was only for the apostles
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:9-13 — The prayer moves from God-ward petitions (name, kingdom, will) to human-ward petitions (bread, forgiveness, protection). This order teaches that prayer begins with God's glory, not our needs. It is a model ("after this manner"), not merely a formula.

adults.question: What does "Hallowed be thy name" mean, and why does it come first?
adults.option_a: To "hallow" means to treat as holy, sacred, and set apart. By placing this first, Jesus teaches that the primary purpose of prayer (and life) is God's glory — His name being honoured, revered, and treated as supremely holy. All other petitions flow from this desire for God's honour
adults.option_b: It is a greeting like "hello"
adults.option_c: It refers to making God's name a secret
adults.option_d: It is a request for God to reveal His name
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:9 — "Hallowed be thy name." The passive voice ("be hallowed") asks God to cause His name to be treated as holy — both by us and in the world. This echoes the third commandment (Exodus 20:7) and Ezekiel 36:23. God's glory is prayer's first and highest concern.

adults.question: What does Jesus mean by "lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven"?
adults.option_a: Jesus contrasts temporal, vulnerable wealth (moth, rust, thieves) with eternal, secure investment in God's kingdom. "Where your treasure is, there will your heart be also" reveals that treasure placement determines heart orientation — giving and kingdom investment redirect the heart toward God
adults.option_b: Christians must have no possessions
adults.option_c: Saving money is always sinful
adults.option_d: Heaven is a literal treasure vault
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:19-21 — "Lay not up... treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal: But lay up... treasures in heaven." The point is not poverty but priority: treasure in heaven means investing in what has eternal value. The heart follows the treasure.

adults.question: What does "the eye is the lamp of the body" (6:22-23) mean in context between the treasures and mammon passages?
adults.option_a: The "single" (haplous) eye means generous, undivided loyalty to God, while the "evil" (poneros) eye means stinginess and divided allegiance. Placed between teachings on treasure (v.19-21) and mammon (v.24), it warns that our spiritual vision — what we focus on — determines whether we walk in light or darkness
adults.option_b: Jesus was giving medical advice about eyesight
adults.option_c: This is about literal night vision
adults.option_d: The eye passage is unrelated to the surrounding context
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:22-23 — "The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light." In Jewish idiom, a "good eye" meant generosity and a "bad eye" meant stinginess (cf. Deuteronomy 15:9; Proverbs 22:9). Jesus connects seeing rightly with living rightly.

adults.question: How does Jesus' teaching on anxiety (6:25-34) address the root cause of worry?
adults.option_a: Jesus identifies the root of worry as insufficient trust in God's fatherly provision — worry is a practical form of functional atheism. He uses lesser-to-greater arguments (birds, lilies, grass) to show that if God cares for lesser creation, He will certainly care for His children. The antidote is not stoicism but faith: "seek ye first the kingdom"
adults.option_b: Worry is merely a psychological problem
adults.option_c: Jesus says all planning is sinful
adults.option_d: Anxiety is unrelated to faith
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:30-33 — "If God so clothe the grass of the field... shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith?... seek ye first the kingdom of God." Jesus diagnoses worry as "little faith" (oligopistia), not a neurological condition. The cure is reorienting priorities toward God's kingdom.

adults.question: What does "Give us this day our daily bread" reveal about the nature of dependence on God?
adults.option_a: It teaches daily dependence — asking for "this day" echoes Israel's daily manna (Exodus 16), where God provided one day at a time. The petition acknowledges that even physical sustenance comes from God, and that faith means trusting Him for today's needs without hoarding for tomorrow
adults.option_b: We should only eat bread and nothing else
adults.option_c: Daily bread refers to spiritual food only
adults.option_d: This prayer was for impoverished people only
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:11 — "Give us this day our daily bread." The Greek "epiousios" (daily) is rare and debated, but the connection to Exodus 16 (daily manna) is clear. God provides not in bulk but daily, cultivating ongoing trust. "Our" (not "my") bread makes it communal.

adults.question: What is the relationship between divine and human forgiveness in verses 14-15?
adults.option_a: Our willingness to forgive others is the evidence (not the cause) of having received God's forgiveness — an unforgiving heart demonstrates that one has not truly grasped grace. Jesus does not teach that human forgiveness earns divine forgiveness, but that receiving God's mercy necessarily transforms how we treat others
adults.option_b: We must forgive others before God will forgive us
adults.option_c: Forgiving others is optional for Christians
adults.option_d: God's forgiveness has no connection to our behaviour
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:14-15 — "If ye forgive men... your heavenly Father will also forgive you." This is not forgiveness-by-works but a kingdom principle: those who have truly received mercy become merciful (cf. 18:21-35, the unforgiving servant). An unforgiving heart contradicts a claim to have received forgiveness.

adults.question: What does "mammon" represent, and why does Jesus personalise wealth as a rival master?
adults.option_a: Mammon (Aramaic mamona, meaning wealth/possessions) is personalised as a competing lord to expose the idolatrous potential of money. Jesus does not say money is evil but that devotion to it is functionally a rival religion — you will either serve God with money as a tool, or serve money with God as an excuse. There is no middle ground
adults.option_b: Mammon was a Canaanite god of wealth
adults.option_c: Money is inherently evil and must be avoided
adults.option_d: Mammon only refers to dishonest money
adults.explanation: Matthew 6:24 — "Ye cannot serve God and mammon." By personifying wealth as "mammon," Jesus elevates it to the status of a rival deity. The word "serve" (douleuein) means to serve as a slave — total allegiance. Dual-allegiance is impossible; one master always wins.

kids.question: What did Jesus say about judging other people?
kids.option_a: Judge not, that ye be not judged
kids.option_c: Only judge people you do not like
kids.explanation: Matthew 7:1 — "Judge not, that ye be not judged."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about asking God for things?
kids.option_a: Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you
kids.option_b: Do not bother God with your requests
kids.option_d: Only grown-ups can ask God for things
kids.explanation: Matthew 7:7 — "Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you."

kids.question: In Jesus' story, what happened to the house built on the rock when the storm came?
kids.option_a: It did not fall because it was founded upon a rock
kids.option_b: It was washed away by the rain
kids.option_c: It was blown down by the wind
kids.explanation: Matthew 7:25 — "And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell not: for it was founded upon a rock."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about trying to remove a speck from someone else's eye?
teens.option_a: First cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother's eye
teens.option_b: You should always point out others' faults first
teens.option_c: Ignore your own faults and fix others
teens.option_d: It is not your business to help anyone
teens.explanation: Matthew 7:5 — "Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother's eye."

teens.question: What golden rule did Jesus give about treating others?
teens.option_a: Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets
teens.option_b: Treat others the way they treat you
teens.option_c: Only be kind to your friends
teens.option_d: Look out for yourself first
teens.explanation: Matthew 7:12 — "Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets."

teens.question: What warning did Jesus give about false prophets?
teens.option_a: Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves — ye shall know them by their fruits
teens.option_b: Accept all prophets without question
teens.option_c: False prophets always look evil
teens.option_d: There are no false prophets
teens.explanation: Matthew 7:15-16 — "Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves. Ye shall know them by their fruits."

teens.question: What will Jesus say to those who call Him "Lord, Lord" but do not do the will of His Father?
teens.option_a: I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity
teens.option_b: Welcome to my kingdom
teens.option_c: You have done a wonderful job
teens.option_d: Come in and rest
teens.explanation: Matthew 7:23 — "And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity."

teens.question: What is the narrow gate that Jesus described?
teens.option_a: The difficult path that leads to life — few find it, while the wide gate and broad way lead to destruction
teens.option_b: The entrance to the temple
teens.option_c: The gate of Jerusalem
teens.option_d: A secret passage in the city walls
teens.explanation: Matthew 7:13-14 — "Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and many there be which go in thereat: Because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it."

adults.question: What does "Judge not, that ye be not judged" prohibit, and what does it not prohibit?
adults.option_a: It prohibits hypocritical, censorious judgment — condemning others for faults you share or worse. It does not prohibit moral discernment (v.6 "give not that which is holy unto the dogs," v.15 "beware of false prophets"), which requires evaluation. Jesus forbids self-righteous condemnation, not spiritual discernment
adults.option_b: All forms of evaluation are forbidden
adults.option_c: Christians must never form opinions about behaviour
adults.option_d: Only ordained ministers may judge
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:1-5 — The context clarifies: the beam/mote illustration (v.3-5) targets hypocrisy, not discernment. Jesus immediately commands evaluation in v.6 (dogs/swine), v.15-20 (know them by fruits), and v.21-23 (genuine vs. false disciples). The prohibition is against playing God, not thinking critically.

adults.question: How does the ask/seek/knock trilogy (7:7-11) build on the Father analogy?
adults.option_a: The three verbs escalate in intensity (ask → seek → knock), and the present tense implies persistence. The argument from lesser to greater follows: if sinful human fathers give good gifts to their children, "how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask him?" God's fatherly generosity exceeds human parenting
adults.option_b: The three words mean the same thing
adults.option_c: This applies only to material requests
adults.option_d: The Father analogy is merely decorative
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:7-11 — "Ask... seek... knock" uses present imperatives = "keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking." The climax (v.11) uses a qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) argument: evil humans → good gifts; how much more → good Father → good things. Prayer is grounded in God's character, not our performance.

adults.question: What does the golden rule (7:12) mean when Jesus says "this is the law and the prophets"?
adults.option_a: Jesus claims that the entire ethical teaching of the Old Testament can be summarised in proactive love — treating others as you would want to be treated. This moves beyond negative formulations ("do not harm") to positive action ("do good"). It is the practical application of the love commandments (22:37-40)
adults.option_b: The golden rule replaces the Ten Commandments
adults.option_c: This was a common pagan saying Jesus borrowed
adults.option_d: It only applies to fellow Jews
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:12 — "This is the law and the prophets." Rabbi Hillel had stated the negative form ("What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbour"). Jesus' positive formulation is more demanding: not merely avoiding harm but actively doing good. It summarises the Sermon's ethical vision.

adults.question: What does "by their fruits ye shall know them" teach about identifying true and false spiritual leaders?
adults.option_a: Character and conduct over time — not miracles, eloquence, or popularity — are the reliable indicators of authentic spiritual authority. Good trees produce good fruit naturally; rotten trees cannot produce good fruit. This test requires patience (fruit takes seasons) and discernment (examining patterns, not isolated incidents)
adults.option_b: External appearances are sufficient to judge
adults.option_c: One miracle proves someone is genuine
adults.option_d: Fruits refers only to numerical growth
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:16-20 — "Ye shall know them by their fruits." The agricultural metaphor implies: (1) time is needed (fruit develops over seasons), (2) the connection is organic (character produces conduct), (3) the test is practical (examine results, not claims). Verses 21-23 show that even supernatural ministry can mask false hearts.

adults.question: What is the shocking implication of "Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name... and in thy name done many wonderful works"?
adults.option_a: People can perform genuine supernatural acts — prophecy, exorcism, miracles — in Jesus' name yet remain unknown to Him personally. Activity for Christ is not the same as relationship with Christ. The criterion is not ministry success but doing "the will of my Father" — personal obedience and genuine faith
adults.option_b: Everyone who calls Jesus "Lord" is automatically saved
adults.option_c: Miracles prove a person is right with God
adults.option_d: Jesus is confused about who serves Him
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:22-23 — "Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name?... And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you." The terrifying word is "many" — not a few self-deceived individuals but many who are active in ministry yet strangers to Christ.

adults.question: How does the wise/foolish builders parable function as the conclusion of the Sermon on the Mount?
adults.option_a: The parable makes the entire Sermon intensely practical — hearing Jesus' words is not enough; only obedient action constitutes a foundation that withstands the storm of judgment. The rock is not intellectual agreement but lived obedience. This framing makes the Sermon not merely ethical teaching but a call to radical life-reorientation
adults.option_b: The parable is about literal construction techniques
adults.option_c: It teaches that all houses eventually fall
adults.option_d: The story is unrelated to the preceding sermon
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:24-27 — "Whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock." Both builders hear; only one does. The difference is not knowledge but obedience. The storm tests which foundation is real.

adults.question: What does "give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine" teach about discernment?
adults.option_a: Even while prohibiting censorious judgment (v.1), Jesus requires wise discrimination — some audiences will trample and attack sacred truth. This verse balances the "judge not" teaching: Christians must neither condemn hypocritically nor share indiscriminately. Holy things must be offered with wisdom, not cast about carelessly
adults.option_b: Jesus is insulting certain ethnic groups
adults.option_c: Christians should never share the gospel
adults.option_d: This contradicts the command not to judge
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:6 — The verse requires the very discernment that v.1 seems to forbid, creating a tension resolved by distinguishing censorious condemnation (forbidden) from wise evaluation (required). The gospel is precious (pearls) and sacred (holy things); it must be handled wisely, not withheld but not forced either.

adults.question: Why were the people "astonished at his doctrine" at the end of the Sermon (7:28-29)?
adults.option_a: Because Jesus taught "as one having authority, and not as the scribes" — scribes always cited prior rabbinical authorities ("Rabbi X says..."), but Jesus spoke with direct, personal authority ("I say unto you"). His claim to authoritatively interpret and deepen the Law was unprecedented and implied divine authority
adults.option_b: They had never heard teaching before
adults.option_c: His voice was unusually loud
adults.option_d: They disagreed with everything He said
adults.explanation: Matthew 7:28-29 — "The people were astonished at his doctrine: For he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes." The scribal method was citation-based; Jesus' method was revelatory. He did not derive authority from tradition but possessed it inherently — a claim only God can make.

kids.question: What did Jesus say to the man with leprosy who asked to be healed?
kids.option_a: I will; be thou clean — and immediately his leprosy was cleansed
kids.option_b: I cannot help you right now
kids.explanation: Matthew 8:3 — "And Jesus put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will; be thou clean. And immediately his leprosy was cleansed."

kids.question: What did Jesus do when a great storm arose on the sea?
kids.option_a: He rebuked the winds and the sea, and there was a great calm
kids.option_d: He asked the disciples to row faster
kids.explanation: Matthew 8:26 — "Then he arose, and rebuked the winds and the sea; and there was a great calm."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about the Roman centurion's faith?
kids.option_a: I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel
kids.option_d: Only people in Israel have faith
kids.explanation: Matthew 8:10 — "When Jesus heard it, he marvelled, and said to them that followed, Verily I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel."

teens.question: What remarkable thing did the centurion say about Jesus' authority?
teens.option_a: He said Jesus only needed to speak the word and his servant would be healed — because he understood authority, having soldiers who obey his commands
teens.option_b: He asked Jesus to come and stay at his house for weeks
teens.option_c: He demanded Jesus prove Himself with a sign
teens.option_d: He questioned whether Jesus had any real power
teens.explanation: Matthew 8:8-9 — "The centurion answered and said, Lord, I am not worthy that thou shouldest come under my roof: but speak the word only, and my servant shall be healed. For I am a man under authority, having soldiers under me."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to the man who wanted to bury his father before following Him?
teens.option_a: Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead
teens.option_b: Go and bury your father first
teens.option_c: Come back in a few weeks
teens.option_d: Bring your father along with you
teens.explanation: Matthew 8:22 — "But Jesus said unto him, Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead."

teens.question: What did the disciples say to each other after Jesus calmed the storm?
teens.option_a: What manner of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him!
teens.option_b: Jesus is just a normal teacher
teens.option_c: Maybe it was just a coincidence
teens.option_d: We should not sail anymore
teens.explanation: Matthew 8:27 — "But the men marvelled, saying, What manner of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him!"

teens.question: What happened when Jesus healed the two demon-possessed men in the country of the Gergesenes?
teens.option_a: The demons went into a herd of swine, and the whole herd ran violently down a steep place into the sea and perished
teens.option_b: The demons simply disappeared
teens.option_c: The men fell asleep peacefully
teens.option_d: Nothing unusual happened
teens.explanation: Matthew 8:32 — "And he said unto them, Go. And when they were come out, they went into the herd of swine: and, behold, the whole herd of swine ran violently down a steep place into the sea, and perished in the waters."

teens.question: Whose mother-in-law did Jesus heal of a fever?
teens.option_a: Peter's mother-in-law — He touched her hand, the fever left her, and she arose and ministered unto them
teens.option_b: John's mother-in-law
teens.option_c: James's mother-in-law
teens.option_d: Andrew's mother-in-law
teens.explanation: Matthew 8:14-15 — "And when Jesus was come into Peter's house, he saw his wife's mother laid, and sick of a fever. And he touched her hand, and the fever left her: and she arose, and ministered unto them."

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus touching the leper (8:3) in the cultural context?
adults.option_a: Touching a leper made a person ceremonially unclean according to Leviticus 13-14.
