But instead of Jesus becoming unclean, the leper becomes clean — Jesus' purity is contagious, reversing the normal direction of contamination. This is a powerful demonstration of the kingdom's in-breaking power over disease and ritual law
adults.option_b: Touching lepers was socially acceptable
adults.option_c: Jesus was unaware of the purity laws
adults.option_d: The touch was accidental
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:3 — "Jesus put forth his hand, and touched him." The touch is deliberate and radical. Under the old covenant, uncleanness was contagious (Haggai 2:11-13); under the new, holiness is contagious. Jesus' touch does not contract impurity — it transmits purity.

adults.question: How does the centurion's understanding of authority reveal a profound Christology?
adults.option_a: The centurion reasons from his own experience: as a man under authority whose word commands obedience, he recognises Jesus as one whose word commands disease, distance, and demons. His faith grasps that Jesus' authority is delegated from God and operates without physical presence — a sophisticated understanding of divine power
adults.option_b: The centurion was simply being polite
adults.option_c: His military background was irrelevant
adults.option_d: He had no real faith in Jesus
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:8-10 — The centurion's logic: "I am a man under authority... I say to this man, Go, and he goeth." He maps his chain-of-command experience onto Jesus' relationship with creation. Just as his word moves soldiers, Jesus' word commands sickness. This reasoning amazed Jesus Himself.

adults.question: What does Jesus' statement that "many shall come from the east and west, and shall sit down with Abraham" predict?
adults.option_a: It predicts Gentile inclusion in the kingdom of God — people from all nations will share in Abraham's covenant blessings, while "the children of the kingdom" (unbelieving Israel) will be cast out. A Gentile centurion's faith triggers this revolutionary declaration about the scope of salvation
adults.option_b: Only Jews will enter the kingdom
adults.option_c: East and west refer to the two halves of Israel
adults.option_d: Abraham will host a literal dinner party
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:11-12 — "Many shall come from the east and west, and shall sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of heaven. But the children of the kingdom shall be cast out." Provoked by a Roman's faith, Jesus announces the universal scope of the gospel.

adults.question: How does Matthew 8:17 connect Jesus' healing ministry to Isaiah 53?
adults.option_a: Matthew quotes Isaiah 53:4 — "Himself took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses" — applying the Suffering Servant prophecy to Jesus' healing ministry. This connection shows that Jesus' healings are not merely compassionate acts but part of His atoning work: He bears humanity's brokenness in all its forms
adults.option_b: Isaiah 53 has nothing to do with healing
adults.option_c: Matthew misquoted Isaiah
adults.option_d: The healings and the cross are unrelated
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:17 — "That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, saying, Himself took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses." Matthew uniquely applies Isaiah 53:4 to the healing ministry (not just the cross), showing that Jesus' entire life is an expression of the Servant's burden-bearing.

adults.question: What does "let the dead bury their dead" mean, and why is it so radical?
adults.option_a: The first "dead" refers to the spiritually dead — those without kingdom urgency. Jesus is not prohibiting filial piety but asserting that following Him takes absolute priority over even the most sacred social obligations. In Jewish culture, burying one's father was the supreme duty; Jesus claims a higher allegiance
adults.option_b: Jesus was being callous about death
adults.option_c: The man's father was not actually dead
adults.option_d: This applies only to Jesus' original disciples
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:22 — "Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead." Honouring parents was the weightiest commandment (Exodus 20:12). For Jesus to supersede it is to claim an authority greater than Torah — a claim only God can make. The cost of discipleship exceeds all other obligations.

adults.question: What does Jesus' ability to calm the storm reveal about His identity?
adults.option_a: In the Old Testament, only God controls the sea (Psalm 89:9, 107:29; Job 38:8-11). By rebuking the wind and sea with a word and producing instant calm, Jesus exercises divine prerogatives over creation. The disciples' question "What manner of man is this?" points toward the answer: He is God incarnate
adults.option_b: Jesus used a natural weather pattern
adults.option_c: The storm was about to end anyway
adults.option_d: The disciples imagined the calm
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:26-27 — Jesus "rebuked the winds and the sea" — the same language used for exorcism. Psalm 89:9: "Thou rulest the raging of the sea: when the waves thereof arise, thou stillest them." Only YHWH rules the sea; Jesus does what only God does.

adults.question: Why does Matthew group ten miracles together in chapters 8-9?
adults.option_a: After presenting Jesus' authoritative teaching (Sermon on the Mount, ch. 5-7), Matthew demonstrates Jesus' authoritative deeds (ten miracles, ch. 8-9). Word and deed together establish Jesus' credentials as Messiah. The miracles are arranged thematically — healings, nature miracles, exorcisms — showing comprehensive authority over disease, creation, and the spiritual realm
adults.option_b: Matthew randomly collected miracle stories
adults.option_c: These miracles happened on the same day
adults.option_d: The grouping has no literary purpose
adults.explanation: Matthew structures his Gospel in alternating blocks of discourse and narrative. Chapters 5-7 (Sermon on the Mount) present Jesus' words; chapters 8-9 present His works. Together they answer: "Is this the Messiah?" (cf. 11:2-6, Jesus' answer to John: "the blind receive their sight...").

adults.question: What is the significance of the Gergesene townspeople asking Jesus to leave after the demons were cast out?
adults.option_a: They preferred their economic livelihood (the swine herd) to the presence of the Son of God — choosing material security over spiritual liberation. The episode reveals that Jesus' transformative power can be unwelcome when it disrupts comfortable systems, even systems built on unclean practices
adults.option_b: They politely invited Him to return later
adults.option_c: They were grateful for the healing
adults.option_d: They did not notice what had happened
adults.explanation: Matthew 8:34 — "The whole city came out to meet Jesus: and when they saw him, they besought him that he would depart out of their coasts." The loss of the swine (their economy) mattered more than the deliverance of two tormented men. Material loss trumped spiritual gain — a persistent human pattern.

kids.question: What did Jesus say to the paralysed man before healing him?
kids.option_a: Son, be of good cheer; thy sins be forgiven thee
kids.option_b: Stand up and walk right now
kids.option_c: You need to see a doctor
kids.explanation: Matthew 9:2 — "And, behold, they brought to him a man sick of the palsy, lying on a bed: and Jesus seeing their faith said unto the sick of the palsy; Son, be of good cheer; thy sins be forgiven thee."

kids.question: What was Matthew doing when Jesus called him to be a disciple?
kids.option_a: Sitting at the tax office collecting taxes
kids.explanation: Matthew 9:9 — "And as Jesus passed forth from thence, he saw a man, named Matthew, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he saith unto him, Follow me. And he arose, and followed him."

kids.question: What did Jesus say when people asked why He ate with sinners?
kids.option_a: They that be whole need not a physician, but they that are sick
kids.option_b: I only eat with good people
kids.option_c: Sinners are not welcome at my table
kids.option_d: I made a mistake coming here
kids.explanation: Matthew 9:12 — "But when Jesus heard that, he said unto them, They that be whole need not a physician, but they that are sick."

teens.question: Why did the scribes accuse Jesus of blasphemy when He forgave sins?
teens.option_a: Because only God can forgive sins — by forgiving the paralytic's sins, Jesus was claiming divine authority, which they considered blasphemy
teens.option_b: Because forgiveness was not allowed on the Sabbath
teens.option_c: Because the man had committed too many sins
teens.option_d: Because Jesus was not a Levite
teens.explanation: Matthew 9:3 — "And, behold, certain of the scribes said within themselves, This man blasphemeth." The scribes correctly understood the implication: forgiving sins is God's exclusive prerogative (Isaiah 43:25; Mark 2:7). Their error was not in theology but in failing to recognise God standing before them.

teens.question: How did Jesus prove He had authority to forgive sins?
teens.option_a: He healed the paralytic — saying the visible miracle (healing) proves the invisible miracle (forgiveness). "That ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins" — then He told the man to arise and walk
teens.option_b: He showed them a scroll from the temple
teens.option_c: He asked God to send a sign from heaven
teens.option_d: He quoted from the Law of Moses
teens.explanation: Matthew 9:6 — "But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins, (then saith he to the sick of the palsy,) Arise, take up thy bed, and go unto thine house."

teens.question: What did Jesus mean by "new wine into new bottles (wineskins)"?
teens.option_a: The new life and teaching Jesus brings cannot be contained in the old forms and traditions — new wineskins stretch with fermenting wine, but old rigid ones burst. The gospel requires new hearts and new ways of living
teens.option_b: He was giving winemaking advice
teens.option_c: Old wineskins are better than new ones
teens.option_d: Wine should never be stored in bottles
teens.explanation: Matthew 9:17 — "Neither do men put new wine into old bottles: else the bottles break, and the wine runneth out, and the bottles perish: but they put new wine into new bottles, and both are preserved."

teens.question: What happened when the woman with a blood issue touched the hem of Jesus' garment?
teens.option_a: She was immediately healed — Jesus said "Daughter, be of good comfort; thy faith hath made thee whole"
teens.option_b: Nothing happened at all
teens.option_c: Jesus scolded her for touching Him
teens.option_d: She had to wait several days to be healed
teens.explanation: Matthew 9:20-22 — "And, behold, a woman, which was diseased with an issue of blood twelve years, came behind him, and touched the hem of his garment... But Jesus turned him about, and when he saw her, he said, Daughter, be of good comfort; thy faith hath made thee whole."

teens.question: What did Jesus say when He saw the multitudes and was moved with compassion?
teens.option_a: The harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few; pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he will send forth labourers into his harvest
teens.option_b: There are too many people to help
teens.option_c: The people do not deserve compassion
teens.option_d: Someone else should take care of them
teens.explanation: Matthew 9:37-38 — "Then saith he unto his disciples, The harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few; Pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he will send forth labourers into his harvest."

adults.question: What is the logical structure of Jesus' argument in healing the paralytic to prove His authority to forgive sins?
adults.option_a: Jesus uses the visible to validate the invisible — both forgiveness and healing require divine power, but healing is publicly verifiable. If Jesus can demonstrably do the harder-to-verify act (forgive sins) through the easier-to-verify act (heal a body), His authority is established. The miracle is evidence, not the essence of His ministry
adults.option_b: The healing was unrelated to the forgiveness
adults.option_c: Jesus was simply showing compassion
adults.option_d: The scribes were convinced by the argument
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:5-6 — "Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and walk?" Both are equally easy to say but equally impossible to do without divine power. The healing proves the forgiveness because the God who can do the visible can also do the invisible.

adults.question: What does the calling of Matthew the tax collector reveal about Jesus' kingdom values?
adults.option_a: Tax collectors were considered traitors (collaborating with Rome) and sinners (profiting from extortion). Jesus deliberately choosing a tax collector as a disciple (and Gospel author) demonstrates that the kingdom accepts those whom religious society rejects, and that no background disqualifies a person from discipleship
adults.option_b: Tax collectors were respected community leaders
adults.option_c: Matthew was already a follower before being called
adults.option_d: Jesus chose Matthew because he was wealthy
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:9 — "He saw a man, named Matthew, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he saith unto him, Follow me." Matthew's immediate response parallels the fishermen (4:20,22). A tax booth was a place of shame; Jesus made it a place of calling.

adults.question: What does "I will have mercy, and not sacrifice" (Hosea 6:6) mean in Jesus' response to criticism about dining with sinners?
adults.option_a: Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 to show that God prioritises compassionate engagement with sinners over ritual purity and religious performance. The Pharisees' concern for ceremonial separateness led them to avoid the very people God wanted to reach. Mercy (hesed) is the heart of God's character, not sacrificial religion
adults.option_b: Sacrifice is no longer required at all
adults.option_c: God does not want any form of worship
adults.option_d: Mercy means tolerating all behaviour
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:13 — "But go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice: for I am not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance." Jesus directs the Pharisees to study Hosea 6:6 — a stinging rebuke suggesting they do not understand their own Scriptures.

adults.question: What does the new wine/wineskins parable teach about the relationship between Jesus' teaching and Judaism?
adults.option_a: Jesus' gospel is not a patch on Judaism but a fundamentally new reality that requires new forms — the old wineskins of Pharisaic practice (fasting schedules, purity regulations, temple-centred religion) cannot contain the dynamic, expanding new covenant. This is not abolition but transcendence
adults.option_b: Jesus was literally discussing winemaking
adults.option_c: Judaism and Christianity are identical
adults.option_d: The old wineskins represent Roman culture
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:16-17 — The old cloth/new garment and old wineskins/new wine parables teach that Jesus' kingdom is not a renovation of Pharisaic religion but a new creation. The forms must match the content: new wine (gospel) demands new wineskins (new covenant community).

adults.question: What is the significance of the woman's twelve-year bleeding and Jairus' daughter being twelve years old?
adults.option_a: The woman had been hemorrhaging for the entire lifetime of the girl — twelve years. One was slowly dying (the woman), the other was suddenly dead (the girl). Jesus' power extends over both chronic suffering and acute death, and both are healed/raised by faith and His word/touch. The number twelve also echoes Israel's twelve tribes
adults.option_b: The number twelve is coincidental
adults.option_c: Matthew invented the connection
adults.option_d: Twelve was simply a round number
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:18-26 — The intercalation (sandwich) of these two stories is deliberate. The hemorrhaging woman was ceremonially unclean for twelve years; Jairus' twelve-year-old daughter had just died. Both represent extremities of human need: perpetual impurity and death itself.

adults.question: What does the title "Son of David" used by the two blind men (9:27) signify?
adults.option_a: It is a messianic title — the blind men recognise Jesus as the promised descendant of David who would restore Israel and heal the afflicted (Isaiah 35:5 "the eyes of the blind shall be opened"). Their physical blindness contrasts with their spiritual sight — they see what the religious leaders miss
adults.option_b: Son of David was a common greeting
adults.option_c: It referred to any descendant of David
adults.option_d: The blind men were confused about Jesus' identity
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:27 — "Two blind men followed him, crying, and saying, Thou Son of David, have mercy on us." "Son of David" was a loaded messianic title (cf. 2 Samuel 7:12-16, Isaiah 11:1). The irony: blind men see Jesus' true identity while sighted Pharisees remain spiritually blind.

adults.question: What does "the harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few" reveal about Jesus' view of the world?
adults.option_a: Jesus sees the multitudes not as a problem but as a harvest — ripe, ready, and abundant. The bottleneck is not unwilling people but insufficient workers. His compassion ("moved with compassion," v.36) drives the mission imperative. Prayer for labourers (v.38) precedes sending labourers (ch. 10), showing mission begins in prayer
adults.option_b: Jesus was overwhelmed by the crowds
adults.option_c: The harvest metaphor refers to agriculture
adults.option_d: Only the twelve can be labourers
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:36-38 — "When he saw the multitudes, he was moved with compassion on them, because they fainted, and were scattered abroad, as sheep having no shepherd." The harvest metaphor shifts from agricultural to eschatological gathering. The labourers are sent by God (v.38) in response to prayer.

adults.question: How does Jesus' response to the Pharisees' accusation that He casts out devils by Beelzebub's power (9:34) set up the later confrontation in chapter 12?
adults.option_a: In 9:34, the Pharisees accuse Jesus of demonic power ("He casteth out devils through the prince of the devils") — Matthew records this without an immediate rebuttal, creating narrative tension. In chapter 12, the same accusation receives a devastating response about the unpardonable sin, showing the escalating conflict between Jesus and the religious establishment
adults.option_b: The accusation is never addressed
adults.option_c: Jesus agreed with the Pharisees
adults.option_d: Chapter 12 discusses a different topic
adults.explanation: Matthew 9:34 — "But the Pharisees said, He casteth out devils through the prince of the devils." This verse foreshadows the climactic confrontation in 12:22-32, where Jesus exposes the absurdity of Satan casting out Satan and warns of blasphemy against the Holy Spirit.

kids.question: How many special disciples (apostles) did Jesus choose and send out?
kids.option_a: Twelve
kids.explanation: Matthew 10:1-2 — "And when he had called unto him his twelve disciples, he gave them power... Now the names of the twelve apostles are these."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about sparrows and how much God cares for us?
kids.option_a: Two sparrows are sold for a farthing, but not one of them falls to the ground without your Father knowing — so do not fear, you are of more value than many sparrows
kids.option_b: God only cares about big animals
kids.option_c: Sparrows are more important than people
kids.option_d: God does not know about sparrows
kids.explanation: Matthew 10:29,31 — "Are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father... ye are of more value than many sparrows."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about confessing Him before men?
kids.option_a: Whosoever shall confess me before men, him will I confess also before my Father which is in heaven
kids.option_b: It does not matter if you talk about Jesus
kids.option_d: Only speak about Jesus in church
kids.explanation: Matthew 10:32 — "Whosoever therefore shall confess me before men, him will I confess also before my Father which is in heaven."

teens.question: What instructions did Jesus give the twelve about where to go on their first mission?
teens.option_a: Go not into the way of the Gentiles, and into any city of the Samaritans enter ye not: but go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel
teens.option_b: Go to every nation on earth
teens.option_c: Only visit the temple in Jerusalem
teens.option_d: Travel to Rome and preach there
teens.explanation: Matthew 10:5-6 — "Go not into the way of the Gentiles, and into any city of the Samaritans enter ye not: But go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel."

teens.question: What did Jesus warn His disciples they would be like among their enemies?
teens.option_a: Sheep in the midst of wolves — so be wise as serpents and harmless as doves
teens.option_b: Lions among sheep
teens.option_c: Eagles in the sky
teens.option_d: Fish in the sea
teens.explanation: Matthew 10:16 — "Behold, I send you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves: be ye therefore wise as serpents, and harmless as doves."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about fearing people versus fearing God?
teens.option_a: Fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell
teens.option_b: Fear everyone equally
teens.option_c: Do not fear anything or anyone
teens.option_d: Fear only the Romans
teens.explanation: Matthew 10:28 — "And fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell."

teens.question: What did Jesus say He came to bring to the earth?
teens.option_a: A sword — not peace. He said following Him would sometimes divide families, because loyalty to Christ must come before all human relationships
teens.option_b: Only peace and harmony everywhere
teens.option_c: A new government for Israel
teens.option_d: Wealth for everyone who believes
teens.explanation: Matthew 10:34-36 — "Think not that I am come to send peace on earth: I came not to send peace, but a sword. For I am come to set a man at variance against his father, and the daughter against her mother."

teens.question: What did Jesus promise about even giving a cup of cold water to a disciple?
teens.option_a: Whosoever shall give a cup of cold water to one of these little ones in the name of a disciple shall in no wise lose his reward
teens.option_b: Only expensive gifts count
teens.option_c: Small acts of kindness do not matter
teens.option_d: Only apostles deserve help
teens.explanation: Matthew 10:42 — "And whosoever shall give to drink unto one of these little ones a cup of cold water only in the name of a disciple, verily I say unto you, he shall in no wise lose his reward."

adults.question: Why did Jesus initially restrict the mission to "the lost sheep of the house of Israel" (10:5-6)?
adults.option_a: The restriction is strategic and temporary — salvation-historical priority demanded that Israel hear first (Romans 1:16 "to the Jew first"). Israel was God's covenant people and the Messiah's natural audience. This limitation is explicitly removed in the Great Commission (28:19 "all nations"), showing progressive revelation
adults.option_b: Jesus did not care about Gentiles
adults.option_c: The restriction was permanent
adults.option_d: It was a suggestion, not a command
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:5-6 — "Go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel." This temporal priority mirrors Paul's pattern in Acts (synagogue first, then Gentiles). Jesus' own ministry crossed ethnic boundaries (8:5-13 centurion, 15:21-28 Canaanite woman), proving the restriction was strategic, not racial.

adults.question: What does "wise as serpents and harmless as doves" mean for Christian witness?
adults.option_a: Disciples need both shrewdness and innocence — serpent-wisdom means being perceptive, strategic, and aware of danger; dove-harmlessness means being pure, non-violent, and transparent. The combination prevents naivety (all dove) and cunning manipulation (all serpent). Christian mission requires both intelligence and integrity
adults.option_b: Christians should be devious like serpents
adults.option_c: Christians should be naive like doves
adults.option_d: The saying applies only to the twelve apostles
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:16 — "Wise as serpents, and harmless as doves." The serpent (Genesis 3:1) represents craftiness; the dove (Genesis 8:11) represents purity. Jesus commands the paradoxical combination: discerning enough to navigate hostility, pure enough never to compromise integrity.

adults.question: What does "the disciple is not above his master, nor the servant above his lord" establish about persecution?
adults.option_a: If Jesus Himself was persecuted and called "Beelzebub," His followers should expect no better treatment — persecution is normative, not exceptional, for faithful discipleship. This reframes suffering from a sign of God's abandonment to a mark of authentic identification with Christ
adults.option_b: Disciples will always be treated better than Jesus
adults.option_c: Persecution only happened in the first century
adults.option_d: Jesus was never actually persecuted
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:24-25 — "The disciple is not above his master... If they have called the master of the house Beelzebub, how much more shall they call them of his household?" The logic: if the world's hostility reached the Master, it will certainly reach the servants.

adults.question: What does the hair-numbering statement ("the very hairs of your head are all numbered") teach about divine providence?
adults.option_a: God's providential care extends to the minutest details of existence — if He tracks every hair on every head and every sparrow's fall, nothing in a disciple's life escapes His notice or care. This exhaustive providence grounds the command "fear not" (repeated three times in vv. 26, 28, 31)
adults.option_b: God only cares about major events
adults.option_c: The statement is hyperbolic and not literal
adults.option_d: Only apostles receive this level of care
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:30-31 — "But the very hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear ye not therefore, ye are of more value than many sparrows." The argument moves from lesser to greater: God's care for sparrows (nearly worthless, two for a farthing) guarantees His infinitely greater care for His children.

adults.question: How does Jesus' statement about bringing "a sword" (10:34) relate to His role as Prince of Peace?
adults.option_a: Jesus brings ultimate peace with God (justification), but in a fallen world, allegiance to Him inevitably creates conflict with those who reject Him. The "sword" is not Jesus' purpose but the result of His divisive claim to absolute lordship — families divide not because Jesus intends discord but because truth divides those who accept it from those who reject it
adults.option_b: Jesus advocates violence
adults.option_c: This contradicts the Beatitudes
adults.option_d: The sword is purely symbolic of nothing
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:34-36 — "I came not to send peace, but a sword." The sword is not a weapon of aggression but a metaphor for division (cf. Micah 7:6, which Jesus quotes). His peace (John 14:27) is not the absence of conflict but reconciliation with God — which provokes conflict with the world.

adults.question: What does "He that findeth his life shall lose it: and he that loseth his life for my sake shall find it" teach about discipleship?
adults.option_a: The paradox of kingdom life: self-preservation leads to spiritual death, while self-sacrifice for Christ's sake leads to true life. Those who cling to earthly security, comfort, and safety at the cost of following Jesus lose everything that matters, while those who surrender everything for Jesus gain what can never be lost
adults.option_b: Christians must seek physical death
adults.option_c: This applies only to martyrs
adults.option_d: The saying is a contradiction with no resolution
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:39 — "He that findeth his life shall lose it: and he that loseth his life for my sake shall find it." The verb "find" (heurisko) implies actively seeking. Those who make self-preservation their goal lose their soul; those who risk everything for Christ find life — real, eternal, abundant life.

adults.question: What does "freely ye have received, freely give" (10:8) establish about the nature of ministry?
adults.option_a: The grace received from Christ must flow outward without commercial transaction — the disciples received their authority, healing power, and message as gifts from Jesus, and must minister in the same spirit of generosity. This principle opposes the monetisation of spiritual power and the commercialisation of the gospel
adults.option_b: Ministers should always charge fees
adults.option_c: Only wealthy disciples can give freely
adults.option_d: This instruction was for the twelve only
adults.explanation: Matthew 10:8 — "Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out devils: freely ye have received, freely give." Grace-based ministry reflects grace-based salvation. The gospel cannot be sold because it was never earned. This principle challenges all forms of religious profiteering.

adults.question: How does chapter 10 function as the second of Matthew's five great discourses?
adults.option_a: Matthew structures his Gospel around five major discourses (chapters 5-7, 10, 13, 18, 23-25), each ending with "when Jesus had finished." Chapter 10 is the Mission Discourse, commissioning the twelve and establishing principles for all future Christian mission — including persecution, dependence on God, fearless witness, and the cost of discipleship
adults.option_b: Matthew has no structured discourses
adults.option_c: Chapter 10 is a collection of random sayings
adults.option_d: There are only two discourses in Matthew
adults.explanation: Matthew 10 — The five-discourse structure (chs. 5-7, 10, 13, 18, 23-25) may parallel the five books of Moses, presenting Jesus as the new Moses/Lawgiver. Chapter 10 moves from initial commission (vv.1-15) to future persecution (vv.16-25) to exhortations for fearless faith (vv.26-42).

kids.question: What did Jesus tell John the Baptist's followers to report back to John in prison?
kids.option_a: The blind receive their sight, and the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, and the deaf hear, the dead are raised up, and the poor have the gospel preached to them
kids.option_b: I am building a great army
kids.option_c: I am too busy to answer
kids.option_d: Tell John to escape from prison
kids.explanation: Matthew 11:4-5 — "Go and shew John again those things which ye do hear and see: The blind receive their sight, and the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, and the deaf hear, the dead are raised up, and the poor have the gospel preached to them."

kids.question: What did Jesus invite weary people to do?
kids.option_a: Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest
kids.option_b: Work harder and you will feel better
kids.option_c: Go to the temple and pay money
kids.explanation: Matthew 11:28 — "Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about His yoke?
kids.option_a: My yoke is easy, and my burden is light
kids.option_b: My yoke is very heavy and hard
kids.option_c: I do not have a yoke
kids.option_d: Only strong people can carry my yoke
kids.explanation: Matthew 11:30 — "For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light."

teens.question: Why did John the Baptist send messengers to ask Jesus "Art thou he that should come?"
teens.option_a: John was in prison and possibly struggling with doubt — Jesus was not overthrowing Rome or judging the wicked as John had expected the Messiah to do, so he sought reassurance
teens.option_b: John did not believe in Jesus at all
teens.option_c: John wanted to publicly embarrass Jesus
teens.option_d: John had never heard of Jesus before
teens.explanation: Matthew 11:2-3 — "Now when John had heard in the prison the works of Christ, he sent two of his disciples, And said unto him, Art thou he that should come, or do we look for another?" John had preached fiery judgment (3:10-12); Jesus was showing mercy. The disconnect prompted his question.

teens.question: What did Jesus say about John the Baptist's importance?
teens.option_a: Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist — yet he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he
teens.option_b: John was a minor prophet of little importance
teens.option_c: John was equal to all other prophets
teens.option_d: John was greater than Jesus
teens.explanation: Matthew 11:11 — "Verily I say unto you, Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist: notwithstanding he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he."

teens.question: Why did Jesus criticise the cities of Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum?
teens.option_a: Because most of His mighty works had been done there yet they did not repent — He said it would be more tolerable for Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom on the day of judgment
teens.option_b: Because they worshipped Roman gods
teens.option_c: Because they refused to pay taxes
teens.option_d: Because they were too far from Jerusalem
teens.explanation: Matthew 11:20-24 — "Then began he to upbraid the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done, because they repented not... it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee."

teens.question: What did Jesus say God had hidden from the wise and revealed to babes?
teens.option_a: The things of the kingdom — the truths of the gospel. God reveals His salvation to the humble and childlike rather than to the intellectually proud
teens.option_b: A secret treasure map
teens.option_c: A new set of commandments
teens.option_d: The location of the temple
teens.explanation: Matthew 11:25 — "I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes."

teens.question: What does Jesus promise those who take His yoke upon them?
teens.option_a: They will find rest for their souls — because His yoke is easy and His burden is light, and He is meek and lowly in heart
teens.option_b: They will become famous and powerful
teens.option_c: They will never have any problems
teens.option_d: They will become wealthy
teens.explanation: Matthew 11:29-30 — "Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light."

adults.question: How does Jesus' answer to John's question (11:4-6) confirm His messiahship without directly saying "yes"?
adults.option_a: Jesus points to Isaiah 35:5-6 and 61:1 being fulfilled — the blind see, lame walk, lepers are cleansed, deaf hear, dead are raised, poor hear the gospel. By listing these specific miracles, He lets Scripture identify Him as the Messiah without making a direct political claim that could be misused
adults.option_b: Jesus was avoiding the question
adults.option_c: Jesus did not think He was the Messiah
adults.option_d: The miracles were coincidental
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:4-5 — Jesus catalogues His works using Isaiah's messianic checklist (Isaiah 35:5-6, 61:1). The final beatitude ("blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me," v.6) gently corrects John: the Messiah's programme includes mercy before judgment. Trust the evidence.

adults.question: What does "he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than John" mean?
adults.option_a: It refers to redemptive-historical position, not personal merit — John was the greatest under the old covenant as the forerunner, but even the least person living under the new covenant (post-cross, post-resurrection, post-Pentecost) has greater spiritual privilege: the indwelling Spirit, completed revelation, and accomplished atonement
adults.option_b: John was morally inferior to all Christians
adults.option_c: The least in the kingdom is literally greater in every way
adults.option_d: Jesus was criticising John
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:11 — "He that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he." Greater in privilege, not in character. John stood at the threshold looking in; new covenant believers live inside the reality John pointed toward. The distinction is epochal, not personal.

adults.question: What does "the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force" (11:12) mean?
adults.option_a: This difficult saying most likely means the kingdom of heaven is being forcefully advanced (through Jesus' ministry) and determined people lay hold of it with intense spiritual earnestness — or alternatively, violent people (like Herod, who imprisoned John) attack the kingdom. Both interpretations acknowledge the kingdom's controversial, contested nature
adults.option_b: Christians should use physical violence
adults.option_c: The kingdom is being conquered by enemies
adults.option_d: Only soldiers can enter the kingdom
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:12 — "The kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force." The Greek allows both "the kingdom is violently attacked" and "the kingdom is forcefully entered." In context (John imprisoned, Jesus' ministry opposed), both meanings are present: the kingdom provokes opposition and demands urgency.

adults.question: What does Jesus' "woe" pronouncements on Galilean cities teach about degrees of judgment?
adults.option_a: Greater revelation brings greater accountability — Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum witnessed more miracles than Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom, so their rejection is more culpable. "More tolerable" implies degrees of judgment based on the light received. Privilege without response increases condemnation
adults.option_b: All judgment is exactly the same
adults.option_c: Tyre and Sidon were righteous cities
adults.option_d: Jesus was being unfair to these cities
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:20-24 — "More tolerable for Tyre and Sidon... for the land of Sodom." The principle: judgment is proportional to revelation. Sodom never saw Jesus' miracles; Capernaum did. Greater light refused = greater condemnation. This principle appears throughout Scripture (Luke 12:48, Hebrews 10:29).

adults.question: What does the statement "All things are delivered unto me of my Father" (11:27) claim about Jesus?
adults.option_a: It is one of the highest Christological claims in the Synoptic Gospels — Jesus claims universal authority delegated by the Father, exclusive mutual knowledge between Father and Son, and sole mediatorial power to reveal the Father. This "Johannine thunderbolt" sounds like John's Gospel dropped into Matthew's narrative
adults.option_b: Jesus was speaking metaphorically about general knowledge
adults.option_c: This claim was added by later editors
adults.option_d: It refers only to teaching authority
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:27 — "All things are delivered unto me of my Father: and no man knoweth the Son, but the Father; neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son, and he to whomsoever the Son will reveal him." The exclusive mutual knowledge between Father and Son implies shared divine nature.

adults.question: How does "Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden" contrast with the Pharisaic system?
adults.option_a: The Pharisees burdened people with endless rules and interpretations (23:4 "heavy burdens and grievous to be borne"). Jesus offers rest instead of religious exhaustion. His "yoke" (a rabbinic term for one's teaching and interpretation of the Law) is easy because it flows from relationship with a meek and lowly Master, not from legalistic performance
adults.option_b: The Pharisees also offered rest
adults.option_c: Jesus was promoting laziness
adults.option_d: This invitation was only for the twelve disciples
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:28-30 — "Come unto me... I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you." A rabbi's "yoke" was his interpretation of Torah. Jesus' yoke replaces the crushing burden of Pharisaic legalism with grace-empowered obedience flowing from a relationship with Christ Himself.

adults.question: What does "no man knoweth the Son, but the Father" imply about Jesus' nature?
adults.option_a: The claim that only God the Father can fully know the Son implies the Son shares the divine nature — only infinite knowledge can comprehend an infinite being. This reciprocal exclusivity (only the Father knows the Son; only the Son knows the Father) places Jesus in a unique ontological category shared only with God
adults.option_b: Jesus was difficult to understand personally
adults.option_c: The disciples knew Jesus fully
adults.option_d: This is about secret information, not nature
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:27 — "No man knoweth the Son, but the Father; neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son." Comprehension of the Son requires the Father's infinite knowledge, implying the Son is infinite. The parallel structure places Father and Son on the same level of being.

adults.question: What does the comparison of "this generation" to children in the marketplace (11:16-19) diagnose about spiritual obstinacy?
adults.option_a: Jesus compares His critics to petulant children who refuse to play any game — they rejected John's asceticism ("he hath a devil") and Jesus' sociability ("a man gluttonous and a winebibber"). The problem is not the messenger's style but the audience's hardness. "Wisdom is justified of her children" means the results of both ministries vindicate God's approach
adults.option_b: Jesus was praising the generation
adults.option_c: Only children were guilty of this behaviour
adults.option_d: The parable is about literal marketplace games
adults.explanation: Matthew 11:16-19 — "We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned unto you, and ye have not lamented." Neither the ascetic (John) nor the social (Jesus) approach satisfied the critics. Their complaint was not about method but about message: repentance and faith.

kids.question: What did Jesus say was lawful to do on the Sabbath?
kids.option_a: It is lawful to do well on the sabbath days — He healed a man's withered hand on the Sabbath
kids.option_b: Nothing at all should be done on the Sabbath
kids.option_c: Only cooking is allowed on the Sabbath
kids.option_d: You must stay in bed all day
kids.explanation: Matthew 12:12 — "Wherefore it is lawful to do well on the sabbath days."

kids.question: What did Jesus say people would give account for on the day of judgment?
kids.option_a: Every idle word that men shall speak
kids.option_b: Only the biggest sins they committed
kids.option_c: Only the things they did, not what they said
kids.explanation: Matthew 12:36 — "But I say unto you, That every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment."

kids.question: What sign did Jesus say He would give the scribes and Pharisees?
kids.option_a: The sign of the prophet Jonas — as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly, so Jesus would be three days and nights in the heart of the earth
kids.option_b: A sign of fire from heaven
kids.option_c: A sign of water from the rock
kids.option_d: A sign of manna from the sky
kids.explanation: Matthew 12:39-40 — "An evil and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given to it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas: For as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth."

teens.question: How did Jesus defend His disciples when the Pharisees accused them of breaking the Sabbath by picking grain?
teens.option_a: He cited David eating the shewbread (1 Samuel 21) and priests working in the temple on the Sabbath — and declared "the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day"
teens.option_b: He apologised to the Pharisees
teens.option_c: He punished the disciples for picking grain
teens.option_d: He agreed with the Pharisees completely
teens.explanation: Matthew 12:3-8 — "Have ye not read what David did... how he entered into the house of God, and did eat the shewbread? ...the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about a kingdom divided against itself?
teens.option_a: Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and every city or house divided against itself shall not stand
teens.option_b: Divided kingdoms are stronger
teens.option_c: Division does not affect kingdoms
teens.option_d: Only human kingdoms can be divided
teens.explanation: Matthew 12:25 — "Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and every city or house divided against itself shall not stand."

teens.question: What is the one sin Jesus said will not be forgiven?
teens.option_a: Blasphemy against the Holy Ghost — speaking against the Holy Ghost shall not be forgiven, neither in this world nor the next
teens.option_b: Telling a small lie
teens.option_c: Missing a day of prayer
teens.option_d: Working on the Sabbath
teens.explanation: Matthew 12:31-32 — "All manner of sin and blasphemy shall be forgiven unto men: but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost shall not be forgiven unto men."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about a tree and its fruit?
teens.option_a: Either make the tree good, and his fruit good; or else make the tree corrupt, and his fruit corrupt: for the tree is known by his fruit
teens.option_b: Bad trees can produce good fruit
teens.option_c: Trees have nothing to do with people
teens.option_d: All trees produce the same fruit
teens.explanation: Matthew 12:33 — "Either make the tree good, and his fruit good; or else make the tree corrupt, and his fruit corrupt: for the tree is known by his fruit."

teens.question: Who did Jesus say are His mother and brothers?
teens.option_a: Whosoever shall do the will of my Father which is in heaven, the same is my brother, and sister, and mother
teens.option_b: Only His biological family members
teens.option_c: Only the twelve disciples
teens.option_d: Only people from Nazareth
teens.explanation: Matthew 12:50 — "For whosoever shall do the will of my Father which is in heaven, the same is my brother, and sister, and mother."

adults.question: How do Jesus' Sabbath controversies (12:1-14) redefine the relationship between law and mercy?
adults.option_a: Jesus establishes the principle "I will have mercy, and not sacrifice" (quoting Hosea 6:6 again, cf. 9:13) — the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. He claims lordship over the Sabbath (v.8), placing Himself above the institution. Human need and divine mercy take precedence over rigid ceremonial observance
adults.option_b: Jesus abolished the Sabbath entirely
adults.option_c: The Pharisees were correct in every detail
adults.option_d: Sabbath rules were never important
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:7-8 — "If ye had known what this meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice, ye would not have condemned the guiltless. For the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day." Jesus does not reject Sabbath but recentres it on its purpose: blessing humanity, not burdening it.

adults.question: What exactly is "blasphemy against the Holy Ghost" and why is it unforgivable?
adults.option_a: In context, it is the deliberate, persistent attribution of the Holy Spirit's work to Satan — seeing Christ's miracles performed by the Spirit and calling them demonic. It is unforgivable not because God's grace has a limit but because this sin, by its nature, permanently rejects the very agent of conviction and repentance (the Spirit), making forgiveness impossible since the person refuses the only means of salvation
adults.option_b: Any sin committed more than once
adults.option_c: Doubting God's existence temporarily
adults.option_d: Using God's name carelessly
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:31-32 — The context is crucial: the Pharisees attributed Jesus' Spirit-empowered exorcism to Beelzebub (v.24). This is not a momentary doubt but a settled, eyes-open rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ. The sin is unforgivable because it refuses the only remedy.

adults.question: How does the sign of Jonah typologically prefigure Jesus' death and resurrection?
adults.option_a: As Jonah was three days and nights in the great fish before emerging alive, so Jesus would be three days in the earth before rising — the only sign Jesus offers a sign-seeking generation is His own death and resurrection. Jonah's emergence brought repentance to Nineveh; Jesus' resurrection brings salvation to the world
adults.option_b: Jonah and Jesus have no connection
adults.option_c: The sign refers to a literal whale appearing
adults.option_d: Three days is an approximate number with no significance
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:40 — "As Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth." The typology: Jonah's "death" and "resurrection" from the fish points to Christ's actual death and bodily resurrection.

adults.question: What does the parable of the returning unclean spirit (12:43-45) warn about?
adults.option_a: Moral reformation without spiritual regeneration is dangerous — merely cleaning up behaviour (the empty, swept house) without being filled with God's Spirit invites worse spiritual bondage. The last state becomes worse than the first because emptiness invites occupation. The application is to "this wicked generation" that rejects Christ
adults.option_b: Demons cannot return once expelled
adults.option_c: Houses should be kept unswept
adults.option_d: This only applies to demon-possessed individuals
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:43-45 — "The last state of that man is worse than the first. Even so shall it be also unto this wicked generation." Self-improvement without Spirit-filling creates a spiritual vacuum. The parable warns Israel: rejecting the Messiah after hearing His message leaves them worse off than before.

adults.question: What does Jesus' claim to be "greater than the temple" (12:6) and "greater than Jonas" and "greater than Solomon" (12:41-42) assert?
adults.option_a: These "greater than" statements make extraordinary claims: Jesus is greater than Israel's holiest place (temple — where God dwelt), greatest prophet (Jonah — who brought Nineveh to repentance), and wisest king (Solomon — whose wisdom drew the queen of Sheba). The cumulative effect is a claim to divine supremacy over all institutions and persons in Israel's history
adults.option_b: Jesus was being modest
adults.option_c: He was only greater in age
adults.option_d: These claims were later additions
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:6,41,42 — "One greater than the temple is here... greater than Jonas... greater than Solomon." Temple = God's dwelling; Jesus is the true temple (John 2:19-21). Jonah = prophet; Jesus is the ultimate prophet. Solomon = wise king; Jesus is the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24).

adults.question: How does Jesus' redefinition of family (12:46-50) reshape the concept of community?
adults.option_a: Jesus creates a new family defined not by blood but by obedience to God's will — spiritual kinship supersedes biological kinship. This is not a rejection of His physical family but a declaration that the kingdom creates a new community where doing the Father's will makes one a brother, sister, and mother of Jesus Himself
adults.option_b: Jesus was disowning His family
adults.option_c: Only the twelve apostles are His family
adults.option_d: Biological family is unimportant to God
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:49-50 — "He stretched forth his hand toward his disciples, and said, Behold my mother and my brethren! For whosoever shall do the will of my Father." The gesture (stretching out His hand) includes all disciples, creating the nucleus of a new community defined by shared obedience rather than shared DNA.

adults.question: What does "out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh" (12:34) teach about the relationship between speech and character?
adults.option_a: Words are diagnostic — they reveal the true condition of the heart, just as fruit reveals the nature of a tree (v.33). A "good treasure" of the heart produces good speech; an "evil treasure" produces evil speech. This is why every "idle word" (argon — careless, unguarded) will be judged: unguarded words are the most revealing of character
adults.option_b: Words have no connection to the heart
adults.option_c: Only written words matter
adults.option_d: Idle words are always forgiven
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:34-37 — "For out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh... By thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned." Words are not arbitrary sounds but the overflow of character. Careless speech is the truest window into the soul.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus quoting Isaiah 42:1-4 (the Servant Song) in Matthew 12:18-21?
adults.option_a: Matthew applies Isaiah's first Servant Song to Jesus to explain why He withdraws and heals quietly (v.15-16) rather than seeking confrontation — the Servant will not quarrel or cry out, will not break a bruised reed, and will bring forth judgment unto victory. Gentile hope is highlighted: "in his name shall the Gentiles trust"
adults.option_b: Isaiah 42 has no messianic meaning
adults.option_c: Jesus was a violent revolutionary
adults.option_d: Matthew invented the quotation
adults.explanation: Matthew 12:18-21 — "He shall not strive, nor cry; neither shall any man hear his voice in the streets. A bruised reed shall he not break." Isaiah's Servant is gentle with the weak, quiet in method, and universal in scope. Jesus' withdrawal from conflict (v.15) fulfils this portrait of patient, compassionate messiahship.

kids.question: In Jesus' story about the sower, where did the good seed land that grew and produced fruit?
kids.option_a: In the good ground
kids.explanation: Matthew 13:8 — "But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold."

kids.question: What did Jesus compare the kingdom of heaven to that starts very small but grows very big?
kids.option_a: A grain of mustard seed — the smallest of all seeds but it grows into the greatest among herbs and becomes a tree
kids.explanation: Matthew 13:31-32 — "The kingdom of heaven is like to a grain of mustard seed... which indeed is the least of all seeds: but when it is grown, it is the greatest among herbs, and becometh a tree."

kids.question: What did the man in Jesus' story do when he found treasure hidden in a field?
kids.option_a: He went and sold all that he had and bought that field because he was so happy
kids.option_b: He left it there and walked away
kids.option_d: He was too afraid to dig it up
kids.explanation: Matthew 13:44 — "The kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath, and buyeth that field."

teens.question: What do the four types of soil represent in the parable of the sower?
teens.option_a: Four different responses to God's word: the path (hardened hearts that don't understand), rocky ground (shallow faith that fades under pressure), thorny ground (faith choked by worldly cares and riches), and good ground (those who hear, understand, and bear fruit)
teens.option_b: Four types of farming techniques
teens.option_c: Four different seasons of the year
teens.option_d: Four geographical regions of Israel
teens.explanation: Matthew 13:18-23 — Jesus explains each soil: the wayside = lack of understanding (v.19), stony places = no root/temporary faith (v.20-21), thorns = cares of the world and deceitfulness of riches (v.22), good ground = understanding and fruit-bearing (v.23).

teens.question: Why did Jesus say He spoke in parables?
teens.option_a: Because to the disciples it was given to know the mysteries of the kingdom, but to others it was not given — parables both reveal truth to seekers and conceal it from the hard-hearted
teens.option_b: Because He could not think of anything else to say
teens.option_c: Because parables were required by Jewish law
teens.option_d: Because stories were easier than miracles
teens.explanation: Matthew 13:11-13 — "Because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given... Therefore speak I to them in parables: because they seeing see not; and hearing they hear not, neither do they understand."

teens.question: In the parable of the wheat and the tares, what did the master tell his servants about the weeds?
teens.option_a: Let both grow together until the harvest, and at harvest time I will tell the reapers to gather the tares first and burn them, then gather the wheat into my barn
teens.option_b: Pull out the weeds immediately
teens.option_c: Ignore the weeds and only water the wheat
teens.option_d: Plant more seeds to overcome the weeds
teens.explanation: Matthew 13:30 — "Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn."

teens.question: What did Jesus compare the kingdom of heaven to regarding a merchant seeking pearls?
teens.option_a: A merchant who found one pearl of great price and sold all he had to buy it — the kingdom is worth everything
teens.option_b: A merchant who collected many cheap pearls
teens.option_c: A merchant who refused to sell anything
teens.option_d: A merchant who only sold pearls to the rich
teens.explanation: Matthew 13:45-46 — "The kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls: Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it."

teens.question: Why did Jesus not do many mighty works in His hometown of Nazareth?
teens.option_a: Because of their unbelief — they were offended by Him, saying "Is not this the carpenter's son?"
teens.option_b: Because He was too tired from travelling
teens.option_c: Because the people were already healed
teens.option_d: Because the Pharisees forbade it
teens.explanation: Matthew 13:57-58 — "And they were offended in him... And he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief."

adults.question: How does the parable of the sower function as the hermeneutical key to understanding all parables?
adults.option_a: Jesus says "Know ye not this parable? and how then will ye know all parables?" (Mark 4:13). The sower parable establishes the fundamental principle: the word (seed) is constant; the variable is the human response (soil). All kingdom parables operate on this axis of human reception of divine truth
adults.option_b: Each parable is completely independent
adults.option_c: The sower parable is the least important
adults.option_d: All parables teach the same lesson identically
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:18-23 — The sower parable is foundational because it addresses why the kingdom message produces different results in different people. The same seed (word) encounters four soils (hearts). Understanding this dynamic — God sows identically but hearts respond differently — unlocks all kingdom teaching.

adults.question: What does Jesus' citation of Isaiah 6:9-10 (13:14-15) teach about judicial hardening?
adults.option_a: Isaiah's "hearing but not understanding, seeing but not perceiving" describes both the consequence of persistent rejection and a divine judicial act — God confirms the hardness people have chosen. Parables serve this dual function: they illuminate the receptive and confirm the unresponsive in their blindness. Grace refused becomes judgment received
adults.option_b: God arbitrarily blinds innocent people
adults.option_c: Isaiah's prophecy is unrelated to parables
adults.option_d: Everyone who hears parables is automatically hardened
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:14-15 — "By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and shall not perceive: For this people's heart is waxed gross." The hardening is judicial (God confirms it) but not arbitrary (they chose it). Parables reveal this division: the same story opens some hearts and closes others.

adults.question: What does the parable of the wheat and tares teach about the church and premature judgment?
adults.option_a: The kingdom on earth will contain both genuine believers (wheat) and counterfeit (tares/darnel, which looks like wheat until harvest). Jesus forbids premature separation — final sorting belongs to God at the end of the age. This counsels patience with the mixed state of the visible church and warns against overzealous purging
adults.option_b: The church should aggressively excommunicate everyone suspicious
adults.option_c: Tares and wheat are indistinguishable even at harvest
adults.option_d: The parable is about literal agriculture
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:24-30,36-43 — Tares (darnel/zizania) are nearly identical to wheat during growth, distinguishable only at harvest. "Lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them" (v.29). The separation at "the end of this world" (v.40) is God's prerogative, not ours.

adults.question: What do the mustard seed and leaven parables teach about the kingdom's growth pattern?
adults.option_a: Both parables emphasise disproportionate growth from small beginnings — the mustard seed (smallest to greatest) shows external expansion, while leaven (working invisibly through dough) shows internal permeation. The kingdom starts inconspicuously but transforms everything it touches, eventually becoming comprehensive
adults.option_b: The kingdom will always remain small
adults.option_c: Leaven always represents evil in Scripture
adults.option_d: These parables describe the kingdom shrinking
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:31-33 — Mustard seed: external, visible, dramatic growth. Leaven: internal, invisible, pervasive influence. Together: the kingdom starts small (a carpenter from Nazareth, twelve disciples) but will ultimately fill the earth. The contrast between humble origins and ultimate triumph is the point.

adults.question: What do the hidden treasure and pearl of great price parables teach about the kingdom's value?
adults.option_a: Both parables teach that the kingdom is worth total sacrifice — but from different angles. The treasure is found accidentally (the kingdom finds some), while the pearl is found after diligent searching (some find the kingdom through seeking). In both cases, the finder joyfully surrenders everything because the kingdom's value infinitely exceeds the cost
adults.option_b: The kingdom can be purchased with money
adults.option_c: Only wealthy people can enter the kingdom
adults.option_d: These parables discourage generosity
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:44-46 — "For joy thereof goeth and selleth all" — the sacrifice is joyful, not grudging. "All that he had" — the cost is total. The parables answer: "What is the kingdom worth?" Answer: everything you have and more. The joy of finding makes the cost seem trivial.

adults.question: What does the parable of the dragnet (13:47-50) teach about the final judgment?
adults.option_a: Like a dragnet that catches every kind of fish and is sorted only when brought to shore, the kingdom message gathers all kinds of people, and separation occurs at "the end of the world" — angels will "sever the wicked from among the just." This parallels the wheat/tares parable but uses fishing imagery (appropriate for Galilean fishermen)
adults.option_b: Fishing is the best way to evangelise
adults.option_c: Only good people hear the gospel
adults.option_d: Judgment happens immediately after conversion
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:47-50 — "The kingdom of heaven is like unto a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind: Which, when it was full, they drew to shore, and sat down, and gathered the good into vessels, but cast the bad away." The dragnet is indiscriminate; the sorting is eschatological.

adults.question: What does "every scribe which is instructed unto the kingdom of heaven is like unto a man that is an householder, which bringeth forth out of his treasure things new and old" mean?
adults.option_a: A kingdom-trained disciple combines the old (Old Testament, traditional teaching) with the new (Jesus' fresh revelation). Continuity and newness coexist: the old is not discarded but reinterpreted in light of Christ. This is Matthew's own self-portrait as a Jewish scribe who became a kingdom disciple
adults.option_b: Old things should be thrown away
adults.option_c: Only new teaching matters
adults.option_d: This describes a literal homeowner
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:52 — "Things new and old." Matthew, a tax collector trained in Jewish tradition, exemplifies this: his Gospel draws on OT prophecy (old) and Jesus' new revelation (new). The kingdom scribe treasures both, seeing how the old prepares for and is fulfilled by the new.

adults.question: Why is Jesus' rejection at Nazareth (13:54-58) placed after the parables discourse?
adults.option_a: The placement illustrates the sower parable in action — Nazareth represents the hard soil. Those most familiar with Jesus (His hometown) are the most offended. "A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country" shows that proximity does not guarantee receptivity. Familiarity breeds contempt, and unbelief limits even divine power ("He did not many mighty works there")
adults.option_b: The placement is random
adults.option_c: Nazareth was Jesus' first preaching location
adults.option_d: The people of Nazareth fully accepted Jesus
adults.explanation: Matthew 13:57-58 — "A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country, and in his own house. And he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief." Jesus' unbelief did not limit His power but His willingness — He will not force miracles on the unwilling.

kids.question: How many people did Jesus feed with five loaves and two fishes?
kids.option_a: About five thousand men, besides women and children
kids.explanation: Matthew 14:21 — "And they that had eaten were about five thousand men, beside women and children."

kids.question: What did Jesus do on the sea during a storm?
kids.option_a: He walked on the water to the disciples' boat
kids.option_b: He swam to the other side
kids.explanation: Matthew 14:25 — "And in the fourth watch of the night Jesus went unto them, walking on the sea."

kids.question: What happened when Peter tried to walk on the water toward Jesus?
kids.option_a: He started to sink when he saw the strong wind and was afraid, but Jesus caught him and said "O thou of little faith, wherefore didst thou doubt?"
kids.option_b: He walked perfectly the whole way
kids.option_c: He decided to stay in the boat
kids.explanation: Matthew 14:30-31 — "But when he saw the wind boisterous, he was afraid; and beginning to sink, he cried, saying, Lord, save me. And immediately Jesus stretched forth his hand, and caught him, and said unto him, O thou of little faith, wherefore didst thou doubt?"

teens.question: Why was John the Baptist beheaded?
teens.option_a: Herod arrested John for rebuking him about taking his brother Philip's wife Herodias. At Herod's birthday feast, Herodias' daughter danced and pleased Herod, who promised her anything. Her mother told her to ask for John's head on a platter
teens.option_b: John had committed a crime against Rome
teens.option_c: John tried to overthrow the government
teens.option_d: John insulted the temple priests
teens.explanation: Matthew 14:3-11 — "For Herod had laid hold on John, and bound him, and put him in prison for Herodias' sake, his brother Philip's wife. For John said unto him, It is not lawful for thee to have her."

teens.question: What did Jesus do before performing the miracle of feeding the five thousand?
teens.option_a: He looked up to heaven, blessed the food, broke the loaves, and gave them to His disciples to distribute
teens.option_b: He asked the people to go find their own food
teens.option_c: He sent the disciples to buy bread in town
teens.option_d: He told the crowd to go home
teens.explanation: Matthew 14:19 — "He commanded the multitude to sit down on the grass, and took the five loaves, and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed, and brake, and gave the loaves to his disciples, and the disciples to the multitude."

teens.question: How many baskets of leftovers were collected after the feeding of the five thousand?
teens.option_a: Twelve baskets full of fragments
teens.option_b: None — everything was eaten
teens.option_c: Two baskets
teens.option_d: Five baskets
teens.explanation: Matthew 14:20 — "And they did all eat, and were filled: and they took up of the fragments that remained twelve baskets full."

teens.question: What did the disciples in the boat say when they saw Jesus walking on the sea?
teens.option_a: They were troubled, saying "It is a spirit" — and they cried out for fear
teens.option_b: They recognised Him immediately and waved
teens.option_c: They ignored Him and kept rowing
teens.option_d: They jumped into the water to meet Him
teens.explanation: Matthew 14:26 — "And when the disciples saw him walking on the sea, they were troubled, saying, It is a spirit; and they cried out for fear."

teens.question: What did the disciples do after Jesus got into the boat and the wind ceased?
teens.option_a: They came and worshipped Him, saying "Of a truth thou art the Son of God"
teens.option_b: They asked Him to leave the boat
teens.option_c: They pretended nothing had happened
teens.option_d: They fell asleep immediately
teens.explanation: Matthew 14:33 — "Then they that were in the ship came and worshipped him, saying, Of a truth thou art the Son of God."

adults.question: What does the feeding of the five thousand reveal about Jesus as the new Moses?
adults.option_a: As Moses provided manna in the wilderness for Israel, Jesus provides bread in a desolate place for the multitude. The parallels are deliberate: a wilderness setting, miraculous provision, the people sitting in orderly groups (cf. Exodus 18:21). Jesus is the greater Moses who feeds not just a nation but all who come to Him
adults.option_b: The feeding has no connection to Moses
adults.option_c: Moses never provided food
adults.option_d: The miracle was purely about compassion
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:15-21 — The wilderness setting (eremos topos), miraculous bread, and satisfied multitude echo the Exodus. The twelve baskets may symbolise the twelve tribes of Israel. Jesus is the prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15) who provides for God's people.

adults.question: What is the significance of twelve baskets of leftovers?
adults.option_a: The twelve baskets likely symbolise the twelve tribes of Israel — there is more than enough provision for all God's people. The abundance (more left over than was started with) demonstrates that God's provision through Christ is not merely sufficient but superabundant. The God who fed Israel in the wilderness does so again through His Son
adults.option_b: Twelve was coincidentally the number of baskets available
adults.option_c: The number has no symbolic meaning
adults.option_d: Twelve baskets represent the twelve apostles' lunch
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:20 — Twelve baskets (kophinoi, Jewish carrying baskets) — one for each tribe, suggesting Jesus provides for all Israel. The feeding points forward to the messianic banquet and backward to wilderness manna. Abundance, not scarcity, characterises kingdom provision.

adults.question: How does Jesus walking on the sea assert His divine identity?
adults.option_a: In Job 9:8, God alone "treadeth upon the waves of the sea." In Psalm 77:19, "Thy way is in the sea, and thy path in the great waters." Jesus performing this act claims the prerogative of YHWH. His words "Be of good cheer; it is I" (ego eimi) echo God's self-revelation "I AM" (Exodus 3:14)
adults.option_b: Walking on water was a common miracle
adults.option_c: It was a shallow sandbar
adults.option_d: Jesus was floating, not walking
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:25-27 — Jesus walks on the sea and says "It is I" (ego eimi). Job 9:8 identifies sea-treading as God's exclusive act. The combination of walking on water + ego eimi constitutes a theophany — God manifesting Himself to His terrified people.

adults.question: What does Peter's experience of walking on water and then sinking teach about faith?
adults.option_a: Peter's faith enabled the impossible (walking on water) but wavered when he shifted focus from Jesus to the threatening circumstances (wind). "O thou of little faith" does not mean no faith but divided focus. The lesson: faith is sustained not by ignoring circumstances but by fixing the gaze on Christ despite them
adults.option_b: Peter had no faith at all
adults.option_c: The wind literally blew Peter down
adults.option_d: Peter was simply too heavy to float
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:29-31 — Peter walked on water (genuine faith), then saw the wind (shifted focus), then sank (fear overtook faith), then cried out (turned back to Jesus), then was rescued (Jesus never let go). The sequence maps the anatomy of faith: fixation on Christ sustains, distraction by circumstances sinks.

adults.question: What is the literary and theological significance of the disciples' confession "Of a truth thou art the Son of God" (14:33)?
adults.option_a: This is the first time the disciples collectively call Jesus "Son of God" in Matthew — provoked by the nature miracles (feeding, sea-walking). It anticipates Peter's great confession in 16:16 and the centurion's declaration in 27:54. The confession emerges from witnessing divine acts that only God can perform
adults.option_b: This confession was routine and unremarkable
adults.option_c: The disciples were quoting a psalm
adults.option_d: Only Peter made this confession
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:33 — "Of a truth thou art the Son of God." Combined with worship (proskunesan), this is more than a compliment. The disciples have witnessed two back-to-back displays of divine power (feeding multitudes, walking on water) and draw the only logical conclusion.

adults.question: What does Herod's murder of John the Baptist foreshadow about Jesus' own fate?
adults.option_a: John's death at the hands of a weak ruler pressured by others prefigures Jesus' death: both are innocent prophets killed by political authorities acting against their own judgment (Herod feared John; Pilate found no fault in Jesus). The narrative structure connects the forerunner's end to the Messiah's coming passion
adults.option_b: John's death is unrelated to Jesus' story
adults.option_c: Herod was a righteous ruler
adults.option_d: John died of natural causes
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:1-12 — John's martyrdom foreshadows Jesus' passion: innocent prophet, unjust trial, weak ruler, political pressure. Matthew places this narrative before Jesus' miracles, creating a contrast: the world kills God's prophets while God multiplies bread and walks on storms.

adults.question: Why does Jesus withdraw to a "desert place apart" (14:13) after hearing of John's death?
adults.option_a: Jesus' withdrawal reflects genuine human grief at His forerunner's murder, but also a pattern of retreating for prayer and preparation (cf. 14:23). The withdrawal is interrupted by the crowds, and Jesus' compassion overcomes His need for solitude — He heals and feeds them. This shows the integration of His human emotion and divine mission
adults.option_b: Jesus was afraid Herod would kill Him
adults.option_c: He needed to plan a political response
adults.option_d: The withdrawal was a strategic military move
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:13-14 — "When Jesus heard of it, he departed thence by ship into a desert place apart: and when the people had heard thereof, they followed him... he was moved with compassion toward them, and he healed their sick." Grief gives way to compassion; personal loss does not halt His mission.

adults.question: What does Jesus' command "Bring them hither to me" (14:18) teach about how God uses inadequate resources?
adults.option_a: The disciples saw the five loaves and two fish as hopelessly inadequate. Jesus' response was not to create food from nothing but to take what little was offered, bless it, and multiply it. God works through surrendered insufficiency, not through human sufficiency — the little that is given to Jesus becomes more than enough
adults.option_b: Jesus only works with large resources
adults.option_c: The food was already sufficient before the miracle
adults.option_d: Jesus created food from nothing
adults.explanation: Matthew 14:17-18 — "We have here but five loaves, and two fishes. He said, Bring them hither to me." The pattern: admit insufficiency, bring what you have to Jesus, let Him bless and multiply. God's power + human offering = abundance. This is the paradigm for all Christian ministry.

kids.question: What did Jesus say makes a person unclean — what goes into the mouth or what comes out?
kids.option_a: Not what goes into the mouth but what comes out of the mouth defiles a man — because evil words come from the heart
kids.option_b: Only what you eat makes you unclean
kids.explanation: Matthew 15:11 — "Not that which goeth into the mouth defileth a man; but that which cometh out of the mouth, this defileth a man."

kids.question: How many people did Jesus feed the second time with seven loaves and a few fishes?
kids.option_a: Four thousand men, besides women and children
kids.explanation: Matthew 15:38 — "And they that did eat were four thousand men, beside women and children."

kids.question: What did the Canaanite woman keep asking Jesus to do?
kids.option_a: Have mercy on her and heal her daughter who was vexed with a devil
kids.option_d: Build a house for her family
kids.explanation: Matthew 15:22 — "Have mercy on me, O Lord, thou Son of David; my daughter is grievously vexed with a devil."

teens.question: Why did Jesus rebuke the Pharisees about their tradition of "Corban"?
teens.option_a: They used a tradition that allowed people to dedicate their money to God (Corban) as an excuse not to financially support their parents — thus making the word of God of none effect by their tradition
teens.option_b: He objected to all religious traditions
teens.option_c: He wanted people to stop giving to the temple
teens.option_d: He thought the Pharisees were too generous
teens.explanation: Matthew 15:5-6 — "But ye say, Whosoever shall say to his father or his mother, It is a gift, by whatsoever thou mightest be profited by me; And honour not his father or his mother, he shall be free. Thus have ye made the commandment of God of none effect by your tradition."

teens.question: What did Jesus list as the evil things that come from the heart?
teens.option_a: Evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, false witness, and blasphemies
teens.option_b: Only anger and hatred
teens.option_c: Only dishonesty
teens.option_d: Only laziness and pride
