teens.explanation: Matthew 15:19 — "For out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, murders, adulteries, fornications, thefts, false witness, blasphemies."

teens.question: What clever answer did the Canaanite woman give Jesus about the children's bread?
teens.option_a: Truth, Lord: yet the dogs eat of the crumbs which fall from their masters' table — even the scraps of God's mercy would be enough to heal her daughter
teens.option_b: She said she deserved a full meal
teens.option_c: She said dogs should eat first
teens.option_d: She walked away offended
teens.explanation: Matthew 15:27 — "And she said, Truth, Lord: yet the dogs eat of the crumbs which fall from their masters' table."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the Pharisees after the disciples reported they were offended?
teens.option_a: Every plant which my heavenly Father hath not planted shall be rooted up. Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind. If the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch
teens.option_b: We should apologise to them
teens.option_c: They are the most important religious leaders
teens.option_d: We must always obey the Pharisees
teens.explanation: Matthew 15:13-14 — "Every plant, which my heavenly Father hath not planted, shall be rooted up. Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind. And if the blind lead the blind, both shall fall into the ditch."

teens.question: How did Jesus respond to the Canaanite woman's persistent faith?
teens.option_a: O woman, great is thy faith: be it unto thee even as thou wilt — and her daughter was made whole from that very hour
teens.option_b: He ignored her request completely
teens.option_c: He told her to come back another day
teens.option_d: He asked the disciples to send her away
teens.explanation: Matthew 15:28 — "Then Jesus answered and said unto her, O woman, great is thy faith: be it unto thee even as thou wilt. And her daughter was made whole from that very hour."

adults.question: How does Jesus' teaching on defilement (15:10-20) revolutionise the biblical purity system?
adults.option_a: Jesus relocates defilement from external contact (food, objects, people) to internal disposition (the heart). This does not abolish the distinction between clean and unclean but reveals its deepest meaning: moral impurity, not ritual impurity, is the real problem. Mark 7:19 explicitly adds "purging all meats," showing the radical implication
adults.option_b: Jesus supported the existing purity laws fully
adults.option_c: External purity is more important than internal
adults.option_d: Jesus only discussed food allergies
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:17-20 — "Whatsoever entereth in at the mouth goeth into the belly... But those things which proceed out of the mouth come forth from the heart; and they defile the man." Jesus inverts the purity paradigm: the heart is the source of defilement, not the stomach.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Jesus' encounter with the Canaanite woman?
adults.option_a: This encounter tests and demonstrates faith that transcends ethnic boundaries. Jesus' initial silence and apparent refusal ("I am not sent but unto the lost sheep of the house of Israel") heighten the tension to showcase the woman's extraordinary faith. Her persistence and humility result in the only person in Matthew whom Jesus praises with "great is thy faith"
adults.option_b: Jesus was genuinely refusing to help Gentiles
adults.option_c: The story shows Jesus changing His mind about Gentiles
adults.option_d: The woman had no real faith
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:22-28 — The woman's faith overcomes every obstacle: silence (v.23), apparent rejection (v.24), and cultural insult (v.26 "dogs"). Her response (v.27) shows humble, tenacious trust — she does not challenge the priority of Israel but claims even the crumbs of God's mercy are sufficient. Jesus commends "great" (megale) faith.

adults.question: What does Jesus' phrase "It is not meet to take the children's bread, and to cast it to dogs" mean, and is it a genuine insult?
adults.option_a: Jesus uses "dogs" (kunarion = little dogs/household pets, not wild street dogs) — a common Jewish metaphor for Gentiles. He is not insulting but testing her faith by articulating the salvation-historical priority. His use of the diminutive form (puppies, not dogs) softens the language, and the woman's response shows she understood the test and passed brilliantly
adults.option_b: Jesus was being deliberately cruel
adults.option_c: The word "dogs" had no cultural significance
adults.option_d: Jesus was quoting a Roman proverb
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:26 — "Kunarion" (little dogs/puppies) is the diminutive, not "kuon" (wild dogs). The household setting (children at table, pets beneath) creates an opening the woman seizes: even pets receive scraps from the master's table. Jesus set up the metaphor knowing she would complete it with faith.

adults.question: How does Jesus' confrontation with the Pharisees over tradition vs. commandment (15:1-9) address the perennial tension between Scripture and tradition?
adults.option_a: Jesus establishes an absolute hierarchy: the commandment of God supersedes all human traditions. When tradition contradicts Scripture (as Corban nullified the fifth commandment), tradition must yield. The quotation of Isaiah 29:13 ("their heart is far from me") diagnoses the root problem: external religious performance without internal devotion
adults.option_b: Tradition is always equal to Scripture
adults.option_c: All traditions are wrong
adults.option_d: The Pharisees had no traditions
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:3,6,8-9 — "Why do ye also transgress the commandment of God by your tradition?... Thus have ye made the commandment of God of none effect by your tradition... in vain they do worship me, teaching for doctrines the commandments of men." The principle: tradition serves Scripture, never supersedes it.

adults.question: What does the feeding of the four thousand add to the feeding of the five thousand?
adults.option_a: The two feedings serve different audiences: the five thousand occurred in Jewish territory (twelve baskets = twelve tribes), while the four thousand occurred in Gentile/Decapolis territory (seven baskets = seven Canaanite nations or completeness). Together, they show Jesus as the bread of life for both Jews and Gentiles
adults.option_b: The second feeding is a doublet of the first
adults.option_c: Jesus forgot He had already done this miracle
adults.option_d: The numbers have no significance
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:32-38 — The location (near Decapolis, Gentile territory), seven baskets (spurides, large hampers vs. twelve kophinoi), and four thousand (vs. five thousand) all differ. Jesus feeds both Israel and the nations, previewing the universal scope of His mission (cf. 28:19).

adults.question: What does Jesus' quote of Isaiah 29:13 reveal about the nature of hypocrisy?
adults.option_a: Hypocrisy is the gap between lip-service and heart-devotion — honouring God with words while the heart is far from Him. The Pharisees' elaborate external practices masked internal distance from God. True worship requires the unity of outward expression and inward reality — when these diverge, worship becomes vain (maten)
adults.option_b: Hypocrisy is a minor character flaw
adults.option_c: Isaiah was not speaking about future religious leaders
adults.option_d: The Pharisees were sincere in their worship
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:8-9 — "This people draweth nigh unto me with their mouth, and honoureth me with their lips; but their heart is far from me. But in vain they do worship me." The diagnosis: the problem is not insufficient religion but insincere religion — proximity of lips without proximity of heart.

adults.question: How does Jesus' statement "Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind" guide responses to false teaching?
adults.option_a: Jesus counsels disengagement from persistent, incorrigible false teachers — not endless debate but strategic withdrawal. The blind-leading-blind metaphor warns that following false teachers leads to shared destruction (both fall into the ditch). Discernment means knowing when to engage and when to leave false teachers to their own devices
adults.option_b: Christians should always debate false teachers
adults.option_c: All religious leaders are blind
adults.option_d: Blindness refers to physical disability
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:14 — "Let them alone: they be blind leaders of the blind." Jesus does not say "argue with them endlessly" but "let them alone." The Pharisees' blindness is wilful (12:24, attributing miracles to Satan) — beyond the point where argument helps. Sometimes the most faithful response is walking away.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jesus healing multitudes on a mountain in Gentile territory (15:29-31)?
adults.option_a: The people "glorified the God of Israel" (15:31) — a phrase that only makes sense if the crowd is predominantly Gentile. Jesus on a mountain healing the lame, blind, dumb, and maimed echoes Isaiah 35:5-6 (messianic healing) extended to the nations. The Messiah's compassion reaches beyond Israel's borders
adults.option_b: The mountain location is insignificant
adults.option_c: All the people were Jewish
adults.option_d: These healings did not really happen
adults.explanation: Matthew 15:30-31 — "And they glorified the God of Israel." This phrase implies a non-Israelite audience — Jews would simply say "God," not "the God of Israel." Jesus' ministry in Gentile territory fulfils Isaiah's vision of the Messiah blessing all nations.

kids.question: What did Peter say when Jesus asked "Who do you say that I am?"
kids.option_a: Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God
kids.option_d: You are one of the prophets
kids.explanation: Matthew 16:16 — "And Simon Peter answered and said, Thou art the Christ, the Son of the living God."

kids.question: What did Jesus say He would build, and the gates of hell would not prevail against it?
kids.option_a: His church
kids.explanation: Matthew 16:18 — "Upon this rock I will build my church; and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it."

kids.question: What did Jesus tell His disciples they must do to follow Him?
kids.option_a: Deny themselves, take up their cross, and follow Him
kids.explanation: Matthew 16:24 — "If any man will come after me, let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to the Pharisees and Sadducees when they asked for a sign from heaven?
teens.option_a: A wicked and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given unto it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas
teens.option_b: He immediately showed them a miracle
teens.option_c: He promised to return with a sign next week
teens.option_d: He agreed they deserved a special sign
teens.explanation: Matthew 16:4 — "A wicked and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given unto it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas."

teens.question: What did Jesus warn His disciples to beware of?
teens.option_a: The leaven (teaching) of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees — their false doctrine that spreads like yeast through dough
teens.option_b: Literal bread and yeast
teens.option_c: The Romans' military strategy
teens.option_d: Fishing in dangerous waters
teens.explanation: Matthew 16:11-12 — "How is it that ye do not understand that I spake it not to you concerning bread, that ye should beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees?... Then understood they how that he bade them not beware of the leaven of bread, but of the doctrine."

teens.question: What did Jesus say to Peter when Peter rebuked Him for predicting His death?
teens.option_a: Get thee behind me, Satan: thou art an offence unto me: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but those that be of men
teens.option_b: Thank you for caring about me, Peter
teens.option_c: You are right, I should not suffer
teens.option_d: Let us discuss this more later
teens.explanation: Matthew 16:23 — "But he turned, and said unto Peter, Get thee behind me, Satan: thou art an offence unto me: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but those that be of men."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about the value of a person's soul?
teens.option_a: What is a man profited, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul? Or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?
teens.option_b: The soul is not very valuable
teens.option_c: Material wealth is more important than the soul
teens.option_d: The soul and the world are worth the same
teens.explanation: Matthew 16:26 — "For what is a man profited, if he shall gain the whole world, and lose his own soul? or what shall a man give in exchange for his soul?"

teens.question: What did Jesus first tell His disciples about His future?
teens.option_a: He must go to Jerusalem, suffer many things from the elders, chief priests, and scribes, be killed, and be raised again the third day
teens.option_b: He would become king of Israel
teens.option_c: He would travel to Rome
teens.option_d: He would retire to Nazareth
teens.explanation: Matthew 16:21 — "From that time forth began Jesus to shew unto his disciples, how that he must go unto Jerusalem, and suffer many things of the elders and chief priests and scribes, and be killed, and be raised again the third day."

adults.question: What does "upon this rock I will build my church" refer to — Peter, his confession, or Christ Himself?
adults.option_a: The most likely interpretation integrates all three: "Peter" (Petros) is the rock (petra) in his representative role as the first to confess Jesus as Messiah. The foundation is Peter's confession of Christ — and by extension, all who make the same confession. The church is built on the Christ whom Peter confessed, with Peter as the foundational spokesperson
adults.option_b: The rock is literally a physical stone
adults.option_c: Only Peter personally is the foundation
adults.option_d: The rock has nothing to do with Peter
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:18 — "Thou art Peter (Petros), and upon this rock (petra) I will build my church." The wordplay connects Peter to the rock. In Ephesians 2:20, the church is built on "apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone." Peter is foundational as a confessor of Christ, not as an independent authority.

adults.question: What are "the keys of the kingdom of heaven" and the power of binding and loosing?
adults.option_a: Keys represent authority to open the kingdom through gospel proclamation (cf. Acts 2, Peter opens the kingdom at Pentecost). "Binding and loosing" was rabbinic language for declaring what is forbidden or permitted — authoritative interpretation and discipline. This authority extends to all apostles (18:18) and is exercised through Spirit-guided community discernment
adults.option_b: Literal metal keys to a gate
adults.option_c: The power to lock people out of heaven permanently
adults.option_d: Only Peter's personal authority with no succession
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:19 — "I will give unto thee the keys of the kingdom of heaven: and whatsoever thou shalt bind on earth shall be bound in heaven." Keys = authority to open/declare (Isaiah 22:22). Binding/loosing = authoritative teaching about what is prohibited/permitted (cf. 18:18, given to the whole church).

adults.question: Why does Jesus call Peter "Satan" immediately after blessing him?
adults.option_a: Peter shifts from being the mouthpiece of the Father (v.17 "flesh and blood hath not revealed it unto thee") to being the mouthpiece of Satan (v.23 "thou savourest not the things that be of God"). The same disciple can speak divine truth one moment and satanic error the next. Peter's temptation mirrors Satan's in chapter 4: offering a crown without a cross
adults.option_b: Jesus was angry at Peter permanently
adults.option_c: Peter was actually possessed by Satan
adults.option_d: Jesus used the word casually as an insult
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:17,23 — The contrast is stunning: "Blessed art thou, Simon" (v.17) → "Get thee behind me, Satan" (v.23). Peter tries to deflect Jesus from the cross — the same temptation Satan offered (4:8-9, kingdoms without suffering). The cross is non-negotiable; any theology without it is satanic.

adults.question: What does "take up his cross" mean in context, before the crucifixion has occurred?
adults.option_a: For Jesus' original audience, a cross was not a religious symbol but a Roman instrument of execution. Taking up one's cross meant being willing to face public shame, suffering, and death — like a condemned criminal carrying his crossbeam to execution. Jesus is demanding willingness to die rather than deny Him
adults.option_b: It meant carrying a piece of wood for exercise
adults.option_c: It was a metaphor for minor inconveniences
adults.option_d: The phrase had no meaning before the crucifixion
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:24 — "Let him deny himself, and take up his cross, and follow me." In the Roman world, condemned prisoners carried their crossbeam to the execution site. Jesus' audience would have understood this as: "Be willing to go to your death for Me." It was shockingly literal, not vaguely metaphorical.

adults.question: How does "flesh and blood hath not revealed it unto thee, but my Father which is in heaven" establish the source of saving faith?
adults.option_a: True recognition of Christ does not originate in human reasoning, education, or intuition but in divine revelation. Peter's confession was not the product of his own insight but a gift from the Father. This establishes that saving faith is supernaturally given, not humanly generated — a theme Paul develops in Ephesians 2:8-9
adults.option_b: Peter figured it out by himself
adults.option_c: The other disciples told Peter what to say
adults.option_d: Jesus was complimenting Peter's intelligence
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:17 — "Blessed art thou, Simon Barjona: for flesh and blood hath not revealed it unto thee, but my Father which is in heaven." The source of Peter's confession is divine revelation. Human reasoning alone ("flesh and blood") cannot produce saving faith — it requires God's illuminating work.

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the word "must" (dei) in verse 21?
adults.option_a: The Greek "dei" (it is necessary/must) indicates divine necessity, not mere inevitability — Jesus' suffering, death, and resurrection are not tragic accidents but the predetermined plan of God. This "divine must" appears throughout the passion predictions and establishes the cross as central to God's purpose, not a plan B
adults.option_b: The word "must" is incidental to the passage
adults.option_c: Jesus was expressing personal desire
adults.option_d: The suffering was optional
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:21 — "From that time forth began Jesus to shew unto his disciples, how that he must (dei) go unto Jerusalem, and suffer... and be killed, and be raised again." The divine necessity (dei) frames the passion as God's predetermined will (cf. Acts 2:23, "determinate counsel"). The cross was Plan A.

adults.question: What does "the gates of hell shall not prevail against it" promise about the church?
adults.option_a: Gates are defensive structures — "the gates of hell (hades)" represents death's power to hold its captives. Jesus promises that death itself cannot withstand the church's advance. This is an offensive promise: the church storms the gates of death, empowered by the resurrection. The church is not merely surviving but conquering
adults.option_b: The church will just barely survive
adults.option_c: Gates of hell represents Satan attacking the church
adults.option_d: The church will need human protection
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:18 — "The gates of hell shall not prevail against it." Gates do not attack; they defend. The imagery is of the church besieging the fortress of death/hades — and winning. Christ's resurrection broke death's gates (cf. Acts 2:24, "it was not possible that he should be holden of it"), and the church continues this victory.

adults.question: How does verse 28 ("some standing here... shall not taste of death, till they see the Son of man coming in his kingdom") relate to the Transfiguration?
adults.option_a: The immediate literary context connects this promise to the Transfiguration (17:1-8), which follows six days later. Peter, James, and John witness Jesus' glory — a preview of His kingdom coming in power. This is a proleptic (anticipatory) vision of the second coming glory, fulfilling the promise without exhausting its ultimate eschatological meaning
adults.option_b: Jesus was predicting the second coming within a generation
adults.option_c: The verse is an unfulfilled prophecy
adults.option_d: This has no connection to the Transfiguration
adults.explanation: Matthew 16:28 — "Some standing here... shall not taste of death, till they see the Son of man coming in his kingdom." Six days later (17:1), three disciples witness Jesus' transfigured glory. Peter himself connects the two in 2 Peter 1:16-18: "We... were eyewitnesses of his majesty" at the Transfiguration.

kids.question: What happened to Jesus on the high mountain in front of Peter, James, and John?
kids.option_a: His face shone like the sun and His clothes became white as the light — He was transfigured
kids.explanation: Matthew 17:2 — "And was transfigured before them: and his face did shine as the sun, and his raiment was white as the light."

kids.question: Which two Old Testament people appeared and talked with Jesus on the mountain?
kids.option_a: Moses and Elias (Elijah)
kids.explanation: Matthew 17:3 — "And, behold, there appeared unto them Moses and Elias talking with him."

kids.question: What did Jesus say about faith the size of a mustard seed?
kids.option_a: If you have faith as a grain of mustard seed, you could say unto this mountain, Remove hence to yonder place, and it shall remove — nothing shall be impossible unto you
kids.option_b: Even mustard seed faith is too small
kids.option_d: Only big faith can do anything
kids.explanation: Matthew 17:20 — "If ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye shall say unto this mountain, Remove hence to yonder place; and it shall remove; and nothing shall be impossible unto you."

teens.question: What did the voice from the cloud say during the Transfiguration?
teens.option_a: This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased; hear ye him
teens.option_b: This is a great prophet, listen carefully
teens.option_c: This is the king of the world
teens.option_d: Do not be afraid of the light
teens.explanation: Matthew 17:5 — "While he yet spake, behold, a bright cloud overshadowed them: and behold a voice out of the cloud, which said, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased; hear ye him."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about Elijah when the disciples asked about him?
teens.option_a: Elias is come already, and they knew him not, but have done unto him whatsoever they listed — referring to John the Baptist
teens.option_b: Elijah never came and never will
teens.option_c: Elijah is still in heaven waiting
teens.option_d: Elijah was just a legend
teens.explanation: Matthew 17:12-13 — "But I say unto you, That Elias is come already, and they knew him not... Then the disciples understood that he spake unto them of John the Baptist."

teens.question: Why could the disciples not cast out the demon from the boy at the foot of the mountain?
teens.option_a: Because of their unbelief — Jesus said this kind does not go out but by prayer and fasting
teens.option_b: Because demons cannot be cast out
teens.option_c: Because the boy did not want to be healed
teens.option_d: Because it was the wrong time of day
teens.explanation: Matthew 17:20-21 — "Jesus said unto them, Because of your unbelief... Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting."

teens.question: How did Jesus pay the temple tax for Himself and Peter?
teens.option_a: He told Peter to go fishing and take the first fish he caught — a coin (a piece of money/stater) would be in its mouth, enough for both of them
teens.option_b: He borrowed money from the disciples
teens.option_c: He asked the temple for a discount
teens.option_d: He refused to pay the tax
teens.explanation: Matthew 17:27 — "Go thou to the sea, and cast an hook, and take up the fish that first cometh up; and when thou hast opened his mouth, thou shalt find a piece of money: that take, and give unto them for me and thee."

teens.question: What did the father of the demon-possessed boy say about his son's condition?
teens.option_a: Lord, have mercy on my son: for he is lunatick, and sore vexed: for ofttimes he falleth into the fire, and oft into the water
teens.option_b: My son has a minor headache
teens.option_c: My son refuses to eat vegetables
teens.option_d: My son does not like going to school
teens.explanation: Matthew 17:15 — "Lord, have mercy on my son: for he is lunatick, and sore vexed: for ofttimes he falleth into the fire, and oft into the water."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Moses and Elijah appearing with Jesus at the Transfiguration?
adults.option_a: Moses represents the Law and Elijah represents the Prophets — the two divisions of the Old Testament. Their appearance with the glorified Jesus shows that the entire Old Testament points to and is fulfilled in Christ. The Father's command "hear ye him" transfers ultimate authority from Law and Prophets to the Son
adults.option_b: They appeared randomly with no significance
adults.option_c: They represented two political parties
adults.option_d: They were angels disguised as Moses and Elijah
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:3-5 — Moses (Law-giver) + Elijah (greatest prophet) + Jesus = the entire revelation of God. The Father's voice ("hear ye him") echoes Deuteronomy 18:15 ("a Prophet... unto him ye shall hearken"), transferring the listen-command from Torah to Christ.

adults.question: How does the Transfiguration function as a preview of the Second Coming?
adults.option_a: Peter himself interprets the Transfiguration as a preview of Christ's "power and coming" (2 Peter 1:16-18). The elements match eschatological imagery: glorified body, bright cloud (Shekinah), heavenly voice, and appearing with OT saints. It temporarily lifts the veil of Jesus' humanity to reveal the glory hidden beneath
adults.option_b: The Transfiguration was a hallucination
adults.option_c: It has no connection to future events
adults.option_d: The Second Coming will be nothing like the Transfiguration
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:1-8 — The Transfiguration reveals what the resurrection and return will fully display: Jesus' inherent divine glory. The "face shining as the sun" anticipates Revelation 1:16; the "bright cloud" recalls the Exodus pillar (Exodus 13:21) and the eschatological cloud (Daniel 7:13).

adults.question: What does Peter's suggestion to build three tabernacles reveal about his misunderstanding?
adults.option_a: Peter wanted to prolong the mountaintop experience and equalise Moses, Elijah, and Jesus as three parallel figures worth commemorating. The Father's interruption ("This is my beloved Son... hear ye him") corrects this by establishing Jesus' supremacy. Moses and Elijah are witnesses; Jesus is the Lord they point to
adults.option_b: Peter's suggestion was perfectly appropriate
adults.option_c: Building tabernacles was a Jewish requirement
adults.option_d: Peter was being deliberately disrespectful
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:4 — "Lord, it is good for us to be here: if thou wilt, let us make here three tabernacles." Peter places Jesus alongside Moses and Elijah (three tabernacles = three equals). God's response rejects this: only one voice matters — "hear ye him." Jesus is not one among equals but the unique Son.

adults.question: What does "this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting" teach about spiritual warfare?
adults.option_a: Some spiritual challenges require deeper dependence on God than others — not all demons (or spiritual obstacles) yield to the same level of faith. Prayer and fasting represent intensified reliance on God, acknowledging that some battles require a depth of communion with God that casual faith does not achieve
adults.option_b: All demons are equally easy to cast out
adults.option_c: Prayer and fasting are merely dietary practices
adults.option_d: This verse was Jesus' only teaching on spiritual warfare
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:21 — "Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting." The disciples' failure (v.16) stemmed from "unbelief" (v.20) — insufficient spiritual preparation. Prayer and fasting are not magical formulas but disciplines that cultivate the deep-faith relationship with God that overcomes the most entrenched spiritual opposition.

adults.question: How does Jesus' second passion prediction (17:22-23) develop beyond the first (16:21)?
adults.option_a: The second prediction adds "The Son of man shall be betrayed into the hands of men" — introducing the element of human betrayal, not just religious opposition. The disciples' sorrow (v.23) shows they are beginning to understand, unlike Peter's rebuke in 16:22. Each prediction deepens the disciples' preparation for the cross
adults.option_b: The two predictions are identical
adults.option_c: The second prediction removes the resurrection
adults.option_d: The disciples ignored this prediction
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:22-23 — "The Son of man shall be betrayed into the hands of men: And they shall kill him, and the third day he shall be raised again. And they were exceeding sorry." "Betrayed" (paradidosthai) is new — adding human treachery to the suffering portrait. The progression: 16:21 (elders/priests/scribes); 17:22 (betrayal); 20:18-19 (Gentiles, crucifixion).

adults.question: What does the temple tax episode (17:24-27) teach about Jesus' identity and His voluntary submission?
adults.option_a: Jesus is the Son of the King (God) and therefore exempt from the temple tax (just as kings' children are exempt from their own taxes). Yet He pays "lest we should offend them" — voluntarily submitting to avoid unnecessary scandal. This reveals both His divine sonship (He is exempt) and His humility (He submits anyway)
adults.option_b: Jesus was required to pay like everyone else
adults.option_c: Jesus refused to pay the tax
adults.option_d: The miracle of the fish-coin is unimportant
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:25-27 — "Of whom do the kings of the earth take custom... of their own children, or of strangers?... Then are the children free." Jesus is the Son of the temple's Owner — therefore free. But He pays to avoid offence. The coin in the fish's mouth demonstrates His lordship over creation even while voluntarily submitting.

adults.question: What does "nothing shall be impossible unto you" (17:20) teach about the relationship between faith and divine power?
adults.option_a: Faith does not generate power independently — it connects the believer to God's omnipotence. Even "mustard seed" faith (the smallest amount) is sufficient because faith's power lies not in its quantity but in the God to whom it is directed. Mountain-moving is not about human willpower but about trusting an all-powerful God
adults.option_b: Larger faith produces more miracles
adults.option_c: Faith is a measurable substance
adults.option_d: This promise is hyperbolic and not real
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:20 — "If ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed... nothing shall be impossible unto you." The mustard seed is proverbially the smallest seed. Even tiny faith moves mountains because it connects to infinite power. The issue is not the size of faith but the size of the God faith trusts.

adults.question: How does the descent from the Transfiguration mountain parallel Moses' descent from Sinai?
adults.option_a: Both Moses and Jesus descend from a mountain of divine glory to find God's people in a state of failure — Moses found the golden calf (Exodus 32), Jesus found the disciples unable to cast out a demon (17:14-16). The parallel reinforces Jesus as the new Moses, but where Moses' face merely reflected glory, Jesus's face was the source of glory
adults.option_b: There is no parallel between the two events
adults.option_c: Moses never went up a mountain
adults.option_d: Jesus was angry like Moses
adults.explanation: Matthew 17:14-16 — Both descents move from divine glory to human failure. Moses: Sinai glory → golden calf crisis. Jesus: Transfiguration glory → demoniac crisis. But Jesus succeeds where Moses' patience failed — He heals the boy and teaches the disciples, demonstrating greater authority and compassion.

kids.question: Who did Jesus say is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?
kids.option_a: Whoever humbles himself like a little child
kids.explanation: Matthew 18:4 — "Whosoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child, the same is greatest in the kingdom of heaven."

kids.question: In Jesus' story about the lost sheep, how many sheep did the shepherd leave to go find the one that was lost?
kids.option_a: Ninety-nine
kids.explanation: Matthew 18:12 — "If a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray?"

kids.question: How many times did Jesus say we should forgive someone?
kids.option_a: Seventy times seven
kids.explanation: Matthew 18:22 — "Jesus saith unto him, I say not unto thee, Until seven times: but, Until seventy times seven."

teens.question: What serious warning did Jesus give about causing a child or a believer to sin?
teens.option_a: It would be better for him to have a millstone hanged about his neck and be drowned in the depth of the sea
teens.option_b: He would receive a small fine
teens.option_c: Nothing would happen to him
teens.option_d: He would miss one day of worship
teens.explanation: Matthew 18:6 — "But whoso shall offend one of these little ones which believe in me, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea."

teens.question: What three steps did Jesus outline for dealing with a brother who sins against you?
teens.option_a: First go and tell him his fault privately; if he does not listen, take one or two witnesses; if he still refuses, tell it to the church
teens.option_b: Ignore it and hope it goes away
teens.option_c: Immediately tell everyone about it
teens.option_d: Report it to the Roman authorities
teens.explanation: Matthew 18:15-17 — "Moreover if thy brother shall trespass against thee, go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone: if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother. But if he will not hear thee, then take with thee one or two more... And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church."

teens.question: What did Jesus promise about two or three gathered in His name?
teens.option_a: For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them
teens.option_b: They will automatically become wealthy
teens.option_c: They will receive two or three gifts
teens.option_d: They should meet only in the temple
teens.explanation: Matthew 18:20 — "For where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them."

teens.question: In the parable of the unforgiving servant, what happened to the servant who refused to forgive?
teens.option_a: His master delivered him to the tormentors until he should pay all that was due — because he would not forgive his fellow servant's small debt after being forgiven an enormous debt himself
teens.option_b: His master praised him for being strict
teens.option_c: Nothing happened because he was the king's favourite
teens.option_d: He was promoted to a higher position
teens.explanation: Matthew 18:34 — "And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the tormentors, till he should pay all that was due unto him."

teens.question: How much did the first servant owe the king in the parable?
teens.option_a: Ten thousand talents — an impossibly enormous sum representing an unpayable debt
teens.option_b: One hundred pence — a small amount
teens.option_c: Ten silver coins
teens.option_d: One gold coin
teens.explanation: Matthew 18:24 — "One was brought unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents." Ten thousand talents was the largest number (myrioi) and the largest unit of currency, representing an astronomical, unpayable debt.

adults.question: How does the child illustration (18:1-5) redefine greatness in the kingdom?
adults.option_a: In a culture where children had no status, rights, or social power, Jesus uses a child as the model of kingdom greatness. The point is not childish naivety but childlike dependence, humility, and lack of social ambition. Kingdom greatness is inversely proportional to self-importance — the humblest is the greatest
adults.option_b: Children were powerful in ancient society
adults.option_c: Jesus valued childish behaviour in adults
adults.option_d: The child was a metaphor for physical smallness
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:3-4 — "Except ye be converted, and become as little children, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven. Whosoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child." Children in first-century culture had zero social leverage. Jesus turns the status hierarchy upside down: lowliness, not ambition, defines greatness.

adults.question: What does the three-step discipline process (18:15-17) establish about church governance and community accountability?
adults.option_a: Jesus establishes a progressive, grace-filled process: (1) private confrontation preserving dignity; (2) small group witnesses ensuring fairness (cf. Deuteronomy 19:15); (3) church community involvement for accountability. The goal is always restoration ("thou hast gained thy brother"), not punishment. Only after all attempts fail does separation occur
adults.option_b: Sinners should be expelled immediately
adults.option_c: Private sin should be publicly announced first
adults.option_d: The church has no authority to discipline
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:15-17 — The sequence is: private → small group → community. Each step expands the circle only as necessary. The goal at every stage is reconciliation: "if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother." Excommunication ("let him be unto thee as an heathen man") is the last resort, not the first response.

adults.question: What does the parable of the unforgiving servant (18:21-35) teach about the relationship between divine and human forgiveness?
adults.option_a: The ten thousand talents (billions in modern currency) vs. one hundred pence (a few dollars) illustrates the infinite disproportion between God's forgiveness of us and our forgiveness of others. Anyone who has truly experienced God's mercy will naturally extend mercy. Refusal to forgive reveals that one has not genuinely grasped grace
adults.option_b: Human forgiveness earns divine forgiveness
adults.option_c: The parable teaches that forgiveness has limits
adults.option_d: The two debts are roughly equivalent
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:32-33 — "I forgave thee all that debt... shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellowservant, even as I had pity on thee?" The ratio is extreme: 10,000 talents vs. 100 denarii = roughly 600,000:1. Our debts against each other are trivial compared to our debt before God.

adults.question: What does "where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them" mean in context?
adults.option_a: In context, this promise relates to church discipline and prayer for reconciliation (vv.15-19), not merely small group worship. Jesus promises His authoritative presence when the community gathers to address sin, pray for unity, and make binding decisions. "In my name" means according to His character and will, not merely using His name as an invocation
adults.option_b: This applies only to worship services
adults.option_c: Jesus is physically present wherever three Christians meet
adults.option_d: The verse has nothing to do with church discipline
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:20 — Read in context, "two or three" connects to the "two or three witnesses" of verse 16 and the "two of you shall agree" of verse 19. Jesus' presence guarantees that Spirit-led community decisions carry heavenly authority (v.18). The promise is about authoritative kingdom presence, not mere companionship.

adults.question: What does the lost sheep parable in this context (vs. Luke 15's context) specifically teach?
adults.option_a: In Luke 15, the lost sheep parable defends Jesus' ministry to sinners against Pharisaic criticism. In Matthew 18, the same parable teaches the church not to despise or neglect "little ones" — vulnerable believers who wander. The application shifts from evangelistic seeking (Luke) to pastoral care (Matthew). "It is not the will of your Father... that one of these little ones should perish"
adults.option_b: The two versions teach identical lessons
adults.option_c: Only Luke's version is authentic
adults.option_d: Matthew added the parable accidentally
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:12-14 — "If a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray... it is not the will of your Father which is in heaven, that one of these little ones should perish." In Matthew's pastoral context (church community), the parable urges care for wandering believers — the church must pursue, not abandon, those who stray.

adults.question: Why does Jesus say "seventy times seven" in response to Peter's question about forgiving seven times?
adults.option_a: Peter thought seven was generous (rabbis taught forgiving three times). Jesus' "seventy times seven" (490 times) is not a literal limit but a symbolic expression of unlimited forgiveness — reversing Lamech's seventy-sevenfold vengeance (Genesis 4:24). Grace replaces revenge; forgiveness has no ceiling
adults.option_b: Jesus meant exactly 490 times
adults.option_c: Peter's suggestion of seven was too many
adults.option_d: Forgiveness should stop after three times
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:21-22 — "Until seventy times seven." In Genesis 4:24, Lamech boasted of seventy-sevenfold revenge. Jesus inverts the principle: where sin multiplied revenge, grace multiplies forgiveness. The number is not a cap but a symbol: forgiveness is unlimited because God's forgiveness of us is unlimited.

adults.question: What does the millstone warning (18:6) teach about the gravity of spiritual harm to vulnerable believers?
adults.option_a: The millstone (mulon onikon — a massive stone turned by a donkey, not a hand-mill) and drowning in the "depth of the sea" represent the worst physical fate imaginable. Jesus says even this is preferable to causing a believer to stumble — implying that the spiritual consequence (hell, v.8-9) is infinitely worse than the most horrific physical death
adults.option_b: The millstone is a light punishment
adults.option_c: Jesus was exaggerating to make a point
adults.option_d: Only hurting children physically triggers this warning
adults.explanation: Matthew 18:6 — "It were better for him that a millstone (mulon onikon) were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea." The donkey-driven millstone was the heaviest common object. Better to die the most horrific death than to face God's judgment for leading a believer into sin.

adults.question: How does Matthew 18 as a whole function as Jesus' discourse on community life (the fourth of five discourses)?
adults.option_a: Chapter 18 is the Discourse on Community, addressing how kingdom citizens relate within the church: humility (vv.1-5), not causing stumbling (vv.6-9), pursuing the wandering (vv.10-14), resolving conflict (vv.15-20), and unlimited forgiveness (vv.21-35). It moves from individual character to communal responsibility, painting a picture of a grace-shaped community
adults.option_b: Chapter 18 has no unifying theme
adults.option_c: It is only about children
adults.option_d: It is mainly about judgment
adults.explanation: Matthew 18 — The discourse unfolds logically: enter the kingdom like a child (humility) → protect the vulnerable (not causing stumbling) → pursue the wandering (lost sheep) → resolve conflict (discipline process) → forgive without limit (unforgiving servant). Every section builds a vision of a community defined by grace, humility, and restoration.

kids.question: What did Jesus say when the disciples tried to stop people from bringing children to Him?
kids.option_a: Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven
kids.option_d: Children should wait until they are older
kids.explanation: Matthew 19:14 — "But Jesus said, Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven."

kids.question: What did the rich young man ask Jesus?
kids.option_a: Good Master, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?
kids.option_b: How can I become the king of Israel?
kids.option_c: Where is the best place to build a house?
kids.option_d: How can I make more money?
kids.explanation: Matthew 19:16 — "And, behold, one came and said unto him, Good Master, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?"

kids.question: What animal did Jesus say was easier to go through the eye of a needle than a rich man entering God's kingdom?
kids.option_a: A camel
kids.explanation: Matthew 19:24 — "It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God."

teens.question: What did Jesus teach about God's original design for marriage?
teens.option_a: God made them male and female from the beginning, and a man shall leave father and mother and cleave to his wife — they are no more twain but one flesh. What God hath joined together, let not man put asunder
teens.option_b: Marriage is only a human tradition
teens.option_c: Divorce is always acceptable for any reason
teens.option_d: Marriage was invented by Moses
teens.explanation: Matthew 19:4-6 — "Have ye not read, that he which made them at the beginning made them male and female... What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder."

teens.question: Why did Moses allow divorce, according to Jesus?
teens.option_a: Because of the hardness of their hearts — but from the beginning it was not so
teens.option_b: Because God wanted people to divorce easily
teens.option_c: Because marriage was not important in Moses' time
teens.option_d: Because women requested it
teens.explanation: Matthew 19:8 — "He saith unto them, Moses because of the hardness of your hearts suffered you to put away your wives: but from the beginning it was not so."

teens.question: What did the rich young man do when Jesus told him to sell all he had and give to the poor?
teens.option_a: He went away sorrowful, for he had great possessions
teens.option_b: He immediately sold everything
teens.option_c: He bargained for a lower amount
teens.option_d: He asked for more time to decide
teens.explanation: Matthew 19:22 — "But when the young man heard that saying, he went away sorrowful: for he had great possessions."

teens.question: What did Jesus promise the disciples who had left everything to follow Him?
teens.option_a: In the regeneration, they would sit upon twelve thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel — and everyone who has left houses, family, or lands for His sake shall receive a hundredfold and inherit everlasting life
teens.option_b: They would receive nothing special
teens.option_c: They would get their old jobs back
teens.option_d: They would become wealthy immediately
teens.explanation: Matthew 19:28-29 — "Ye which have followed me, in the regeneration when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory, ye also shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel."

teens.question: What surprising statement did Jesus make about who can be saved?
teens.option_a: With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible
teens.option_b: Only rich people can be saved
teens.option_c: Salvation is easy for everyone
teens.option_d: No one can ever be saved
teens.explanation: Matthew 19:26 — "But Jesus beheld them, and said unto them, With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible."

adults.question: How does Jesus' teaching on marriage (19:3-9) go beyond the Pharisees' debate about acceptable grounds for divorce?
adults.option_a: The Pharisees debated whether divorce was permitted for "any cause" (Hillel) or only for sexual immorality (Shammai). Jesus bypasses both schools by going back to creation (Genesis 1:27, 2:24) — establishing God's original intent as the standard. Divorce was never God's design but a concession to human sinfulness
adults.option_b: Jesus sided with the school of Hillel
adults.option_c: Jesus abolished marriage
adults.option_d: The Pharisees never discussed divorce
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:3-8 — The Pharisees ask about grounds for divorce; Jesus redirects to God's design for marriage. By citing Genesis before Deuteronomy, He establishes creation-intent as normative: "from the beginning it was not so." Divorce is a concession to fallenness, not a component of the created order.

adults.question: What does the rich young ruler episode reveal about the relationship between wealth and salvation?
adults.option_a: The young man's attachment to wealth prevented him from following Jesus — his possessions possessed him. Jesus does not condemn wealth per se but exposes its idolatrous potential: when wealth becomes the thing you cannot surrender, it has become your god. The camel/needle metaphor shows that self-salvation through riches is humanly impossible
adults.option_b: Wealthy people cannot be saved
adults.option_c: Poverty guarantees salvation
adults.option_d: Jesus approved of the young man's choice
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:21-24 — "If thou wilt be perfect, go and sell that thou hast... he went away sorrowful." The young man's sorrow reveals his true master. The command was diagnostic: "sell all" exposed the idol. The camel/needle imagery means human impossibility — only divine grace can save the wealthy or anyone else (v.26).

adults.question: What does "with men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible" (19:26) teach about the nature of salvation?
adults.option_a: Salvation is impossible through human effort, religious achievement, or moral reform — it requires a divine miracle. Even the disciples are "exceedingly amazed" (v.25), asking "Who then can be saved?" Jesus' answer applies to everyone: no one can save themselves. Salvation is entirely God's work of grace
adults.option_b: Salvation is achievable through sufficient effort
adults.option_c: Only the rich find it impossible
adults.option_d: God makes it possible by lowering the standard
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:25-26 — "Who then can be saved? ...With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible." The disciples' shock reveals that everyone assumed wealth was a sign of God's favour. If the blessed-by-God rich cannot be saved by their advantages, no one can be saved by human means. Salvation is entirely a divine work.

adults.question: What does the "regeneration" (palingenesia) in 19:28 refer to?
adults.option_a: The Greek "palingenesia" (literally "new birth/genesis again") refers to the cosmic renewal at Christ's return — the new heavens and new earth (Isaiah 65:17; Revelation 21:1). In this renewed creation, the apostles will share in Christ's rule, judging (governing/leading) the restored twelve tribes of Israel
adults.option_b: It refers to personal conversion
adults.option_c: It means the temple being rebuilt
adults.option_d: It describes a political revolution
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:28 — "In the regeneration when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory." Palingenesia appears only here and in Titus 3:5 (personal regeneration). Here it is cosmic — the restoration of all things (Acts 3:21). The twelve thrones correspond to the twelve tribes, fulfilling Israel's ultimate restoration.

adults.question: Why does Jesus initially respond "Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God" (19:17)?
adults.option_a: Jesus is not denying His goodness or deity but challenging the young man's casual use of "good." Either "good" is a superficial compliment (in which case it is hollow) or it implies deity (in which case the young man should follow through on its implications). Jesus forces him to take his own words seriously
adults.option_b: Jesus was denying His divinity
adults.option_c: Jesus was being humble and nothing more
adults.option_d: Jesus did not understand the question
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:17 — "Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God." Jesus does not say "I am not good" but asks "Why do you call me good?" The implied logic: if goodness belongs to God alone, and you rightly call me good, what does that say about who I am? The question is Christological, not a denial.

adults.question: What does "many that are first shall be last; and the last shall be first" (19:30) summarise?
adults.option_a: The great reversal is a kingdom principle: those with worldly advantage (wealth, status, religious credentials) who refuse to follow Jesus will end up last, while those who sacrifice everything — the socially last — will be first. This principle reverses human merit systems and grounds kingdom status in grace and costly obedience, not in privilege
adults.option_b: The chronologically first arrivals will be served last
adults.option_c: This statement has no practical meaning
adults.option_d: Only applies to the twelve apostles
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:30 — "Many that are first shall be last; and the last shall be first." This aphorism bookends chapter 19-20 (repeated in 20:16), framing the rich young ruler's failure (first → last) and the disciples' sacrifice (last → first). The kingdom inverts every human ranking.

adults.question: How do Jesus' words about eunuchs "for the kingdom of heaven's sake" (19:12) relate to celibacy and calling?
adults.option_a: Jesus acknowledges three categories: congenital eunuchs, those made eunuchs by others, and those who voluntarily embrace celibacy for the kingdom. This legitimises both marriage and celibacy as valid callings, with celibacy being a special gift ("he that is able to receive it, let him receive it") — not a superior state but a particular vocation
adults.option_b: Jesus commanded all followers to be celibate
adults.option_c: Only married people can serve God
adults.option_d: This verse has no practical application
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:12 — "There be eunuchs, which have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven's sake. He that is able to receive it, let him receive it." Jesus neither mandates celibacy nor devalues it. It is a gift (cf. 1 Corinthians 7:7) for those called to it, freeing them for undivided kingdom service.

adults.question: What does Jesus' blessing of the children (19:13-15) teach about the nature of receiving the kingdom?
adults.option_a: The kingdom belongs to those who come as children do — with empty hands, no achievements, no bargaining chips, and complete dependence. This stands in direct contrast to the rich young ruler who approaches with a "What good thing shall I do?" mindset. Children receive gifts; adults try to earn them. The kingdom is received, not achieved
adults.option_b: Children are sinless and therefore worthy
adults.option_c: Only literal children can enter the kingdom
adults.option_d: The blessing was a social custom with no theology
adults.explanation: Matthew 19:14 — "Of such is the kingdom of heaven." The juxtaposition with the rich young ruler (vv.16-22) is deliberate: children receive freely; the ruler wants to earn his way. The kingdom is a gift to the empty-handed (children), not a reward for the accomplished (the ruler).

kids.question: In Jesus' story about the workers in the vineyard, how much did every worker receive at the end of the day?
kids.option_a: One penny (denarius) — even those who only worked for one hour received the same as those who worked all day
kids.option_b: The early workers received much more
kids.explanation: Matthew 20:9-10 — "And when they came that were hired about the eleventh hour, they received every man a penny. But when the first came, they supposed that they should have received more; and they likewise received every man a penny."

kids.question: What did the mother of James and John ask Jesus?
kids.option_a: She asked that her two sons might sit on Jesus' right and left hand in His kingdom
kids.option_b: She asked Jesus to heal her sons
kids.option_d: She asked Jesus to visit her home
kids.explanation: Matthew 20:21 — "She saith unto him, Grant that these my two sons may sit, the one on thy right hand, and the other on the left, in thy kingdom."

kids.question: What did Jesus say He came to do?
kids.option_a: The Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many
kids.option_b: He came to be served by everyone
kids.option_c: He came to become a wealthy ruler
kids.option_d: He came to build a great palace
kids.explanation: Matthew 20:28 — "Even as the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many."

teens.question: What lesson does the parable of the workers in the vineyard teach?
teens.option_a: God's generosity is not based on how long you've served — He gives the same grace to those who come late as to those who have been faithful since the beginning. "The last shall be first, and the first last"
teens.option_b: You should always try to work the longest
teens.option_c: God pays based on hours worked
teens.option_d: Those who come first are always rewarded more
teens.explanation: Matthew 20:1-16 — "So the last shall be first, and the first last: for many be called, but few chosen." The parable illustrates grace: God's generosity does not follow human calculations of merit. The landowner's response: "Is it not lawful for me to do what I will with mine own? Is thine eye evil, because I am good?"

teens.question: What "cup" did Jesus say James and John would drink?
teens.option_a: The cup of suffering — they would indeed share in His suffering and persecution
teens.option_b: A cup of celebration at a feast
teens.option_c: A cup of wine at a wedding
teens.option_d: A cup of water from the well
teens.explanation: Matthew 20:22-23 — "Ye shall drink indeed of my cup, and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with: but to sit on my right hand, and on my left, is not mine to give, but it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared of my Father."

teens.question: How did Jesus define greatness for His disciples?
teens.option_a: Whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister (servant); and whosoever will be chief among you, let him be your servant
teens.option_b: The greatest should be the most powerful
teens.option_c: Greatness means having the most followers
teens.option_d: The oldest disciple is automatically the greatest
teens.explanation: Matthew 20:26-27 — "But it shall not be so among you: but whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister; And whosoever will be chief among you, let him be your servant."

teens.question: What did the two blind men near Jericho cry out to Jesus?
teens.option_a: Have mercy on us, O Lord, thou Son of David
teens.option_b: Give us money for food
teens.option_c: Tell us the way to Jerusalem
teens.option_d: Make us your disciples
teens.explanation: Matthew 20:30 — "And, behold, two blind men sitting by the way side, when they heard that Jesus passed by, cried out, saying, Have mercy on us, O Lord, thou Son of David."

teens.question: What did the third passion prediction add that the earlier ones did not?
teens.option_a: It specifically mentions being delivered to the Gentiles, being mocked, scourged, and crucified — and rising again the third day
teens.option_b: It removed the prediction of resurrection
teens.option_c: It predicted Jesus becoming king instead
teens.option_d: It said Jesus would escape death
teens.explanation: Matthew 20:18-19 — "The Son of man shall be betrayed unto the chief priests and unto the scribes, and they shall condemn him to death, And shall deliver him to the Gentiles to mock, and to scourge, and to crucify him: and the third day he shall rise again."

adults.question: What does the parable of the vineyard workers teach about grace versus merit?
adults.option_a: The parable attacks the principle that God rewards proportionally to human effort. The grumbling of the early workers exposes the transactional mindset ("we deserve more"). Grace is God's sovereign generosity: "Is thine eye evil, because I am good?" Those offended by equal grace reveal that they serve for reward, not love
adults.option_b: God is unfair to long-term workers
adults.option_c: The parable teaches labour economics
adults.option_d: Only the last-hired deserve reward
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:15 — "Is it not lawful for me to do what I will with mine own? Is thine eye evil, because I am good?" The parable shatters merit-based religion. Grace is not unfair — it is generous. The "evil eye" (Jewish idiom for stinginess/envy) diagnoses the problem: they begrudge God's goodness to others.

adults.question: How does Jesus' "ransom" statement (20:28) establish the doctrine of substitutionary atonement?
adults.option_a: The word "ransom" (lutron) means a price paid to liberate captives. "For" (anti) means "in the place of" — Jesus gives His life as a substitute for many. This single verse contains substitution (His life for ours), liberation (ransom from sin/death), and scope (many, not all, yet sufficient for all). It is the most concentrated atonement statement in Matthew
adults.option_b: Jesus' death was merely an example of sacrifice
adults.option_c: Ransom implies God owed a payment to Satan
adults.option_d: The verse does not address atonement
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:28 — "To give his life a ransom (lutron) for (anti) many." Lutron (ransom price) + anti (in place of) = substitutionary atonement. Jesus' life is the price that liberates "many" (cf. Isaiah 53:11-12 "shall my righteous servant justify many... he bare the sin of many").

adults.question: What does Jesus' third passion prediction (20:17-19) reveal about His foreknowledge and willingness?
adults.option_a: This is the most detailed prediction: betrayal, condemnation, delivery to Gentiles, mocking, scourging, and crucifixion — yet followed by resurrection. Jesus approaches His death with full knowledge and deliberate intention. He is not a victim but a willing sacrifice, predicting every detail because He is sovereignly walking into the Father's plan
adults.option_b: Jesus was guessing about what might happen
adults.option_c: The prediction was added after the events
adults.option_d: Jesus was uncertain about the outcome
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:17-19 — The progression of detail: 1st prediction (16:21) = suffer, be killed, rise; 2nd (17:22-23) = betrayed, killed, rise; 3rd (20:18-19) = betrayed, condemned, delivered to Gentiles, mocked, scourged, crucified, rise. Each adds specificity, showing Jesus' complete foreknowledge.

adults.question: How does the mother of Zebedee's sons' request (20:20-21) reveal persistent misunderstanding of Jesus' kingdom?
adults.option_a: Even after three passion predictions, the disciples (through James and John's mother) still envision a political kingdom with positions of honour and power. The request exposes the gap between Jesus' servant-kingdom and the disciples' glory-kingdom. Jesus redirects: the path to thrones goes through suffering ("can ye drink of my cup?")
adults.option_b: The request was perfectly appropriate
adults.option_c: The mother was acting alone without her sons' knowledge
adults.option_d: Jesus had promised these specific positions
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:20-22 — The request for right-hand and left-hand seats (positions of highest honour) comes immediately after the third passion prediction — showing the disciples heard "kingdom" but filtered out "suffering." Jesus responds with the cup metaphor: glory comes through suffering, not instead of it.

adults.question: What does "the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister" establish as the model for Christian leadership?
adults.option_a: Jesus rejects the Gentile leadership model (authority as domination, v.25) and establishes servant leadership — where authority is exercised through service. The ultimate proof is His death: the highest authority (Son of man) performs the lowest service (giving His life). Christian leadership is measured by sacrifice, not by status
adults.option_b: Christian leaders should seek power and honour
adults.option_c: Servanthood is only for non-leaders
adults.option_d: Jesus' example applies only to Him
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:25-28 — "It shall not be so among you" marks a categorical break from worldly power structures. Gentile rulers "exercise dominion" (katakurieuousin) and "exercise authority" (katexousiazousin) — both verbs suggest oppressive control. Jesus' alternative: the greatest serves the most, following His own example of ransom-giving death.

adults.question: What is the "cup" Jesus refers to, and what does it signify in Old Testament imagery?
adults.option_a: In the OT, "the cup" is a recurring metaphor for God's wrath and judgment (Psalm 75:8; Isaiah 51:17,22; Jeremiah 25:15). Jesus' cup is the cup of divine wrath against sin that He will drink on the cross in humanity's place. James and John, in saying "we are able," do not grasp the full weight of what they agree to
adults.option_b: The cup is a literal drinking vessel
adults.option_c: The cup represents celebration and joy
adults.option_d: The cup metaphor is unique to the New Testament
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:22 — "Are ye able to drink of the cup that I shall drink of?" In Gethsemane (26:39), Jesus prays to have "this cup" removed. The cup = God's wrath (Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15-16). The disciples will indeed suffer (Acts 12:2, James martyred), but only Jesus drinks the cup of divine judgment in full.

adults.question: How does the healing of the two blind men (20:29-34) serve as a transitional passage to the Passion narrative?
adults.option_a: The blind men see what the sighted disciples miss — they call Jesus "Son of David" (messianic title), persist despite being told to be quiet, and receive mercy. Their healed sight contrasts with the disciples' spiritual blindness about Jesus' suffering. They then "followed him" — becoming model disciples as Jesus enters Jerusalem for the final week
adults.option_b: The healing has no connection to surrounding passages
adults.option_c: The blind men represent the Pharisees
adults.option_d: This is a duplicate of another healing story
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:30-34 — The blind men's faith-sight exposes the disciples' faith-blindness. They confess "Son of David" (the Davidic Messiah) as Jesus heads to Jerusalem. Their healed following frames the Triumphal Entry: those who truly see Jesus follow Him, even toward the cross.

adults.question: What does "many be called, but few chosen" (20:16) mean in the parable's context?
adults.option_a: The "many called" are all who hear the gospel invitation (like all the workers hired throughout the day). The "few chosen" does not mean God arbitrarily selects some and rejects others, but that many who are called reject grace by insisting on merit-based reward (like the grumbling workers). The chosen are those who accept grace as grace, without demanding that God conform to their calculations
adults.option_b: God only calls a few people
adults.option_c: Everyone who is called is automatically chosen
adults.option_d: This phrase applies to a different parable
adults.explanation: Matthew 20:16 — "Many be called, but few chosen." In context, the "called" are all the workers; the "chosen" are those who accept the landowner's generous terms. The first-hired workers are "called" but disqualify themselves by demanding merit-based payment. Grace accepted = chosen; grace resented = self-excluded.

kids.question: What animal did Jesus ride into Jerusalem on?
kids.option_a: A donkey (and a colt, the foal of a donkey)
kids.explanation: Matthew 21:7 — "And brought the ass, and the colt, and put on them their clothes, and they set him thereon."

kids.question: What did the people shout as Jesus rode into Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: Hosanna to the Son of David: Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord; Hosanna in the highest
kids.option_b: Go away, we do not want you
kids.explanation: Matthew 21:9 — "And the multitudes that went before, and that followed, cried, saying, Hosanna to the Son of David: Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord; Hosanna in the highest."

kids.question: What did Jesus do when He found people buying and selling in the temple?
kids.option_a: He overthrew the tables of the moneychangers and said "My house shall be called the house of prayer; but ye have made it a den of thieves"
kids.option_b: He bought some things for Himself
kids.option_d: He asked them to sell cheaper items
kids.explanation: Matthew 21:12-13 — "And Jesus went into the temple of God, and cast out all them that sold and bought in the temple, and overthrew the tables of the moneychangers... My house shall be called the house of prayer; but ye have made it a den of thieves."

teens.question: What Old Testament prophecy did the Triumphal Entry fulfil?
teens.option_a: Zechariah 9:9 — "Tell ye the daughter of Sion, Behold, thy King cometh unto thee, meek, and sitting upon an ass, and a colt the foal of an ass"
teens.option_b: Isaiah 53 about the suffering servant
teens.option_c: Daniel 7 about the Son of Man
teens.option_d: Micah 5:2 about Bethlehem
teens.explanation: Matthew 21:4-5 — "All this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying, Tell ye the daughter of Sion, Behold, thy King cometh unto thee, meek, and sitting upon an ass, and a colt the foal of an ass."

teens.question: What happened to the fig tree Jesus cursed?
teens.option_a: It withered away immediately — Jesus used it as a lesson about faith
teens.option_b: It grew more leaves
teens.option_c: It produced fruit the next day
teens.option_d: Nothing happened at all
teens.explanation: Matthew 21:19-20 — "And presently the fig tree withered away. And when the disciples saw it, they marvelled, saying, How soon is the fig tree withered away!"

teens.question: In the parable of the two sons, which son did the will of the father?
teens.option_a: The first son who initially said "I will not" but afterward repented and went — representing tax collectors and sinners who initially disobeyed God but later repented
teens.option_b: The second son who said "I go, sir" but did not actually go
teens.option_c: Both sons did the father's will equally
teens.option_d: Neither son obeyed the father
teens.explanation: Matthew 21:29-31 — "He answered and said, I will not: but afterward he repented, and went... Whether of them twain did the will of his father? They say unto him, The first."

teens.question: What is the parable of the wicked husbandmen about?
teens.option_a: God (the landowner) sent prophets (servants) to Israel (the vineyard), but the leaders (husbandmen) rejected and killed them. Finally He sent His Son (Jesus), whom they also killed. The kingdom would be taken from them and given to others
teens.option_b: A lesson about good farming practices
teens.option_c: A story about paying fair wages
teens.option_d: A tale about building a vineyard
teens.explanation: Matthew 21:33-43 — "He sent unto them his servants... they beat one, and killed another... Last of all he sent unto them his son... They caught him, and cast him out of the vineyard, and slew him... The kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and given to a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof."

teens.question: What did Jesus say about faith and prayer in connection with the fig tree?
teens.option_a: If ye have faith, and doubt not, ye shall not only do this which is done to the fig tree, but also if ye shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; it shall be done
teens.option_b: Faith has nothing to do with prayer
teens.option_c: Only special people can pray with power
teens.option_d: Prayer works without any faith
teens.explanation: Matthew 21:21 — "If ye have faith, and doubt not, ye shall not only do this which is done to the fig tree, but also if ye shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; it shall be done."

adults.question: Why does Jesus ride a donkey rather than a horse into Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: A horse was the animal of war (Revelation 19:11); a donkey was the animal of peace (Judges 5:10, kings rode donkeys in peacetime). By choosing a donkey, Jesus fulfils Zechariah 9:9 and declares Himself a peaceful king — claiming royal authority while rejecting military conquest. He enters as the Prince of Peace, not a warrior
adults.option_b: Donkeys were more expensive than horses
adults.option_c: Jesus could not find a horse
adults.option_d: Riding a donkey was shameful and Jesus was being humble
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:5 — "Thy King cometh unto thee, meek, and sitting upon an ass." Zechariah 9:9 describes a king who is "just, and having salvation; lowly" — the opposite of a conquering general on a warhorse. Jesus' choice of mount is a theological statement about the nature of His kingdom.

adults.question: What does the temple cleansing (21:12-13) reveal about Jesus' authority and the state of worship?
adults.option_a: By cleansing the temple, Jesus acts with prophetic authority (cf. Malachi 3:1-3, "the Lord... shall suddenly come to his temple"). The Court of the Gentiles had become a marketplace, preventing Gentile worship. Jesus' quotation combines Isaiah 56:7 ("house of prayer for all nations") with Jeremiah 7:11 ("den of robbers"), accusing the leaders of converting worship space into commerce
adults.option_b: Jesus objected to all commerce
adults.option_c: The temple cleansing was a minor incident
adults.option_d: Only the Romans were selling in the temple
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:12-13 — "My house shall be called the house of prayer; but ye have made it a den of thieves." Isaiah 56:7 includes "for all people" — the Gentile court was being misused. Jeremiah 7:11's "den of robbers" implies they used the temple as a hideout between acts of injustice. Jesus attacks both the commercial corruption and the exclusion of Gentiles.

adults.question: What does the cursing of the fig tree symbolise?
adults.option_a: The fig tree represents Israel: outward appearance of vitality (leaves) but no fruit (deeds of righteousness). Jesus' cursing enacts a prophetic judgment on fruitless religion — specifically the temple establishment that looked religious but bore no spiritual fruit. The withering demonstrates that God judges appearance without substance
adults.option_b: Jesus was merely hungry and disappointed
adults.option_c: The fig tree represents Rome
adults.option_d: It was a random act of power
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:19 — "And when he saw a fig tree in the way, he came to it, and found nothing thereon, but leaves only." The fig tree is a common OT symbol for Israel (Jeremiah 8:13, Hosea 9:10, Micah 7:1). Leaves without fruit = outward religion without genuine righteousness. The temple cleansing (vv.12-13) and fig tree (vv.18-19) interpret each other.

adults.question: How does the parable of the two sons (21:28-32) trap the religious leaders?
adults.option_a: The Pharisees identify the first son (who initially refused but later obeyed) as doing the father's will — then Jesus applies it: tax collectors and harlots who initially lived in disobedience but repented enter the kingdom, while religious leaders who said "I go, sir" (outward compliance) but never truly obeyed are shut out
adults.option_b: The Pharisees refused to answer
adults.option_c: The parable was too complex to understand
adults.option_d: Jesus praised both sons equally
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:31 — "Verily I say unto you, That the publicans and the harlots go into the kingdom of God before you." The trap: the Pharisees correctly identify the obedient son, then discover they are the disobedient one. Their verbal religion ("I go, sir") without actual repentance is worse than initial refusal followed by genuine change.

adults.question: What does the parable of the wicked husbandmen (21:33-46) predict about Israel's leadership and the kingdom's transfer?
adults.option_a: The parable allegorically traces Israel's rejection of the prophets (servants) and the coming murder of the Son (Jesus). "The kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and given to a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof" predicts the transfer of kingdom responsibility from Israel's leaders to the church (a "nation" from every nation, 1 Peter 2:9)
adults.option_b: The kingdom remains with the same leaders
adults.option_c: The vineyard represents Rome
adults.option_d: The son in the parable represents John the Baptist
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:43 — "The kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and given to a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof." The vineyard = Israel (Isaiah 5:1-7). The husbandmen = leaders. The servants = prophets. The son = Jesus. The "nation" (ethnos) = the church, a fruit-bearing people drawn from all nations (1 Peter 2:9).

adults.question: How does the stone rejected by the builders (Psalm 118:22-23) connect the two parables in chapter 21?
adults.option_a: The rejected stone links the wicked husbandmen parable (they kill the son) to the cornerstone imagery — the very stone Israel's leaders reject becomes the foundation of God's new building. Those who "fall on this stone" (stumble over Christ in this life) will be broken in repentance; those on whom it "falls" (in judgment) will be crushed
adults.option_b: The stone has no messianic significance
adults.option_c: Only the parable of the husbandmen matters
adults.option_d: The stone represents the temple building
adults.explanation: Matthew 21:42-44 — "The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner." Psalm 118:22 was sung at Passover — Jesus quotes it during Passover week. The "builders" (Israel's leaders) rejected Jesus, but God made Him the cornerstone. The stone imagery adds judicial consequences to the parable's warning.

adults.question: What does "Hosanna to the Son of David" reveal about the crowd's expectations?
adults.option_a: "Hosanna" means "save now" (from Psalm 118:25-26, a Passover psalm). "Son of David" is the messianic title. The crowd hails Jesus as the Davidic Messiah who will deliver Israel — but their expectations are political liberation, not spiritual salvation. The same crowd will shout "Crucify him" when Jesus fails to meet their political expectations
adults.option_b: Hosanna was a common greeting with no significance
adults.option_c: The crowd fully understood Jesus' mission
adults.option_d: Son of David was not a messianic title
