adults.option_a: The bribe-story is self-refuting: if the guards were asleep, they could not know what happened (sleeping witnesses are no witnesses). The very need to fabricate an alternative explanation confirms that the tomb was indisputably empty — the question was never whether the tomb was empty but how it became empty. The leaders' cover-up is evidence for the resurrection
adults.option_b: The bribery successfully explained the empty tomb
adults.option_c: The guards were telling the truth about the theft
adults.option_d: There was no guard posted
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:13-15 — "Say ye, His disciples came by night, and stole him away while we slept." The absurdity: (1) sleeping soldiers = capital offence in Rome; (2) sleeping witnesses cannot testify to what happened; (3) the disciples had no motive (they were hiding in fear). The cover story's weakness strengthens the resurrection claim.

adults.question: What does "teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you" establish about the content of discipleship?
adults.option_a: Discipleship is not merely conversion (baptism) but ongoing instruction in obedience to everything Jesus taught. "All things" (panta) includes the Sermon on the Mount, kingdom parables, community ethics, and the cross-shaped life. The Great Commission produces not merely believers but obedient followers who embody Jesus' teaching in all of life
adults.option_b: Only the Ten Commandments need to be taught
adults.option_c: Baptism alone is sufficient for discipleship
adults.option_d: Teaching is optional after conversion
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:20 — "Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you." The verbs create a process: go → make disciples → baptise → teach to obey. Discipleship is comprehensive ("all things"), ethical ("observe/obey"), and Christocentric ("whatsoever I have commanded"). The five discourses of Matthew provide the curriculum.

adults.question: How does "Lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world" bookend the entire Gospel of Matthew?
adults.option_a: Matthew begins with "Emmanuel... God with us" (1:23) and ends with "I am with you alway" (28:20). The entire Gospel is framed by divine presence. The incarnation promise (God with us in Christ) is now universalised: the risen Christ is with all disciples, in all places, until the end of the age. The presence that began in a manger continues through the church
adults.option_b: The two promises are unrelated
adults.option_c: Emmanuel only referred to a political title
adults.option_d: Jesus' presence ended at the ascension
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:20 — "Lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world." The inclusio (literary bookend): Emmanuel (1:23) → "I am with you" (28:20). The Gospel begins and ends with the same promise: God's presence with His people. The risen Christ fulfils the Emmanuel promise permanently and universally.

adults.question: What does the women's response to the risen Jesus (holding His feet and worshipping Him) confirm about the resurrection?
adults.option_a: Physical touch (holding His feet) confirms a bodily resurrection — not a vision, hallucination, or spiritual appearance. Their worship (proskunesan) is appropriate only for deity — the resurrection vindicates all of Jesus' divine claims. The combination of physical contact and worship establishes both the reality of the resurrection body and the propriety of worshipping the risen Christ
adults.option_b: They were touching a spirit
adults.option_c: The women were hallucinating
adults.option_d: Worship was premature at this point
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:9 — "They came and held him by the feet, and worshipped him." Physical grasp (feet = tangible body) + worship (proskunesan = divine honour). The bodily resurrection is not a metaphor: the same body that was crucified is now risen, touchable, and rightly worshipped. Thomas later makes the same discovery (John 20:28).

adults.question: How does the Great Commission reverse the initial restriction of chapter 10?
adults.option_a: In Matthew 10:5-6, Jesus restricted the mission to "the lost sheep of the house of Israel." In 28:19, the restriction is removed: "all nations" (panta ta ethne). The death and resurrection have changed the salvation-historical situation — the gospel is no longer limited by ethnic or geographical boundaries. What was strategically restricted is now universally released
adults.option_b: The restriction was never real
adults.option_c: The Great Commission only applies to the apostles
adults.option_d: Chapter 10 and 28 contradict each other
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:19 vs. 10:5-6 — The contrast is deliberate: "Go not into the way of the Gentiles" (10:5) → "Go... teach all nations" (28:19). The cross and resurrection break down the dividing wall (Ephesians 2:14). Israel's priority (10:5-6) was not exclusion but sequence: Israel first, then all nations.

adults.question: How does Matthew 28 function as the climax and summary of Matthew's entire Gospel?
adults.option_a: Chapter 28 resolves every major theme: (1) Jesus is the risen King (fulfilling the Davidic promise of ch. 1); (2) He has universal authority (fulfilling "all power," Daniel 7:14); (3) the mission extends to all nations (fulfilling the Abrahamic promise, Genesis 12:3); (4) Trinitarian baptism reveals the fullness of God; (5) "I am with you" fulfils Emmanuel (1:23). Matthew's Gospel moves from promise to fulfilment, from Israel to all nations, from incarnation to cosmic lordship
adults.option_b: Chapter 28 is a simple ending with no connections to earlier chapters
adults.option_c: Only the resurrection matters, not the commission
adults.option_d: The chapter was added by a later editor
adults.explanation: Matthew 28:16-20 — Every element connects backward: "all authority" ← Daniel 7 (referenced in 26:64); "all nations" ← Abraham (1:1) and the Magi (2:1-12); "baptizing... teaching" ← Jesus' own baptism (3:13-17) and five discourses; "I am with you" ← Emmanuel (1:23). The Great Commission is the Gospel of Matthew in miniature.
